proposals.title: GNN-LIME: Model-Agnostic Explanations for Graph Neural Networks via Local Interpretable Surrogates
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are increasingly used for high-stakes predictions in domains like computational biology and health, where understanding model decisions is critical for trust and scientific discovery. Existing gradient-based explanation methods (e.g., GNNExplainer) are inherently coupled to the target model's architecture and training, limiting their applicability to proprietary, complex, or non-differentiable GNNs. A model-agnostic approach that operates purely on input-output pairs is needed to provide flexible, post-hoc explanations without accessing internal model states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by treating a pre-trained GNN as a black-box function and adapting local surrogate explanation frameworks (like LIME) to the graph domain, we can generate faithful and human-interpretable explanations for node, link, and graph-level predictions without requiring gradient information.
proposals.method: We will develop GNN-LIME, a framework that constructs explanations by perturbing the input graph structure (e.g., randomly adding/removing edges or masking node features) around a target instance and querying the black-box GNN. A locally faithful, interpretable surrogate model (such as a sparse linear model or a simple tree) will be trained on these perturbed samples and their corresponding predictions. For scalability and biological relevance, we will focus perturbation strategies on biologically plausible modifications (e.g., mimicking single-cell perturbation experiments) and employ efficient subgraph sampling techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorously evaluated, model-agnostic explanation tool specifically designed for GNNs in biological networks, enabling interpretability for any GNN architecture. It will deliver a practical software package and benchmark its explanations against gradient-based methods on established single-cell and protein-interaction network inference tasks, assessing both fidelity and biological plausibility.
proposals.novelty: While LIME exists for images and text, its adaptation to graph-structured data without gradient access is non-trivial and unexplored. The key novelty lies in defining a meaningful perturbation model for graphs that preserves fundamental topological properties while enabling efficient and biologically grounded explanation generation.

proposals.title: HeteroHyperExplainer: A Framework for Explaining Predictions on Heterogeneous Graphs and Hypergraphs
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are increasingly applied to complex, multi-relational systems in computational biology and health, such as protein-protein interaction networks with multiple node/edge types (heterogeneous graphs) and multi-way relationships like protein complexes (hypergraphs). However, the leading explanation method, GNNExplainer, is designed for simple homogeneous graphs, limiting interpretability in these critical domains. Providing faithful explanations for predictions on these complex structures is essential for building trust and extracting actionable biological evidence from single-cell and protein network models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generalized explanation framework, which explicitly models the distinct semantics of node/edge types in heterogeneous graphs and higher-order interactions in hypergraphs, can produce more faithful and interpretable explanations than applying homogeneous graph explainers directly.
proposals.method: We will extend the GNNExplainer framework by formulating a novel, unified mask optimization objective. For heterogeneous graphs, we will learn type-specific feature and edge masks over a metapath-aggregated computation graph. For hypergraphs, we will learn masks on hyperedges and their constituent nodes, building upon recent spectral hypergraph convolution operators. The framework will be evaluated on benchmark tasks from our prior work, including inference from single-cell perturbation data and protein function prediction, using quantitative fidelity metrics and qualitative case studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated model-agnostic explainer for heterogeneous graphs and hypergraphs, enabling deeper analysis of GNNs in biomedicine. It will contribute a new benchmark for explainability on complex relational data and offer open-source tools to help researchers uncover the physical and functional drivers behind model predictions in network biology.
proposals.novelty: While existing explainers treat all nodes and edges uniformly, our method explicitly accounts for the structured heterogeneity and higher-order interactions inherent to real-world biological systems, a non-trivial extension beyond the homogeneous graph assumption of GNNExplainer.

proposals.title: C-GNNExplainer: Counterfactual Graph Generation for Interpretable Predictions in Biological Networks
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are increasingly used for high-stakes predictions in biological systems, such as inferring protein-protein interaction networks from single-cell perturbation data. However, existing post-hoc explainers like GNNExplainer provide factual attributions but fail to answer critical 'what-if' questions essential for scientific reasoning. Integrating formal counterfactual reasoning from causal inference is necessary to generate actionable, minimal interventions on a graph that would alter a model's prediction, moving from descriptive to mechanistic explanations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that generating minimal, plausible counterfactual graph edits—such as adding or removing specific edges or nodes—can provide more actionable and robust explanations for GNN predictions in biological networks than existing factual attribution methods.
proposals.method: We will develop C-GNNExplainer, a framework that treats the input graph as a structural causal model. Building on GNNExplainer and recent advances in causal inference for graphs, we will formulate counterfactual generation as a constrained optimization problem. The method will use a GNN-based generator to propose minimal graph edits (e.g., perturbing edges corresponding to protein interactions) and a discriminator to ensure the counterfactuals remain within the data manifold, validated using benchmark single-cell perturbation datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, causally-grounded framework for explaining GNNs, providing biologists with interpretable 'what-if' scenarios for network predictions. It will advance the field of explainable AI for graphs beyond feature attribution, offering a tool for hypothesis generation in systems biology and network medicine.
proposals.novelty: While counterfactual explanations are established for tabular and image data, their application to graph-structured data, particularly for explaining GNNs in biological contexts with rigorous manifold constraints, remains largely unexplored and non-trivial.

proposals.title: SCALEX: Scalable Explanation of Web-Scale Networks via Adaptive Subgraph Sampling
proposals.motivation: GNNExplainer has become a cornerstone for interpreting Graph Neural Network (GNN) predictions, but its reliance on optimizing over a local subgraph makes it computationally intractable for web-scale graphs with billions of nodes and edges. As GNNs are increasingly applied to massive real-world systems in health (e.g., protein interaction networks) and the built environment (e.g., infrastructure networks), there is a critical need for post-hoc explanation methods that scale. Current sampling methods for large graphs, like random walks or neighborhood sampling, are not designed for the joint optimization of explanation fidelity and computational efficiency required by explanation tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adaptive, multi-stage sampling strategy, which prioritizes nodes based on both structural importance and the GNN's gradient signals, can produce faithful explanations for individual predictions with sub-linear time complexity, enabling GNNExplainer to scale to massive graphs.
proposals.method: We will develop SCALEX, a framework that first uses a fast, proxy model (e.g., a simplified linear GNN) to score and select candidate nodes for an explanation subgraph via adaptive PageRank or influence scores. This initial candidate set will then be refined using a second-stage, budgeted optimization that incorporates gradient information from the target GNN, similar to GRAPHSAGE's sampling but with an explanation-aware loss. We will integrate techniques from scalable evidence collection in knowledge graphs and benchmark the approach using large-scale single-cell network inference data and web-scale social or citation graphs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first practical method for generating instance-level explanations for predictions on web-scale graphs, extending the interpretability toolkit to modern industrial-scale GNN applications. We will provide a rigorous benchmark comparing sampling strategies for explanation fidelity versus speed, establishing best practices for scalable graph explainability.
proposals.novelty: While sampling for GNN training is well-studied, sampling specifically for the *explanation* optimization loop is novel, requiring a hybrid approach that balances connectivity, model sensitivity, and computational budget in a way standard neighborhood sampling does not. This bridges the gap between scalable graph processing and the nuanced requirements of interpretability.

proposals.title: InverseGNN: Training Graph Neural Networks with Explanation-Guided Regularization for Interpretable Biological Network Inference
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are powerful for modeling biological systems, such as protein-protein interaction or single-cell perturbation networks, but their predictions are often black-boxes, limiting trust and biological insight. Post-hoc explanation methods like GNNExplainer or PGExplainer are applied after training, which can lead to explanations that are not faithful to the model's true reasoning. This is critical in health and biology, where evidence-based, interpretable models are needed to understand cellular activity and guide experiments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that directly incorporating explanation constraints, such as sparsity and fidelity, as a regularization term during GNN training will yield models that are inherently more interpretable without sacrificing predictive performance on tasks like network inference from single-cell data.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel training framework where the loss function combines a standard task-specific loss (e.g., link prediction for biological networks) with a regularization term derived from an explanation objective. Specifically, we will adapt a parameterized explainer (e.g., akin to PGExplainer) to be co-trained with the primary GNN. The explainer will generate edge masks during training, and a regularization term will enforce desired properties (e.g., mask sparsity and high mutual information between the masked subgraph and predictions). We will benchmark this on single-cell perturbation datasets, comparing against GNNs trained without explanation regularization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new paradigm for building inherently interpretable GNNs, moving beyond post-hoc analysis. It will provide a method to train models whose learned representations align more directly with human-understandable evidence, which is crucial for scientific discovery in biology. We will release a framework and benchmarks for explanation-constrained training applicable to network inference and other graph-based tasks in health.
proposals.novelty: While explanation methods are typically applied post-training, the inverse approach of using explanation constraints to *guide* the primary model's training from scratch is largely unexplored. This flips the standard paradigm, aiming to bake interpretability directly into the model's parameters rather than attempting to extract it afterward.

proposals.title: Sublinear Densification in Biological Networks: A Generative Framework for Single-Cell Perturbation Graphs
proposals.motivation: Network models for complex systems, from social to biological, often assume densification (superlinear edge growth). However, recent single-cell perturbation datasets, which map cellular responses to interventions, suggest that underlying regulatory networks may densify sublinearly or not at all, reflecting constrained biological wiring. This discrepancy challenges existing inference methods and our understanding of network evolution in resource-limited physical systems like cells.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single-cell perturbation response graphs exhibit sublinear edge growth due to physical and energetic constraints on cellular interaction capacity, and that generative models incorporating these constraints will more accurately recover true regulatory networks from perturbation data.
proposals.method: We will develop a constrained graph generative model, building on pre-2026 methods like Graph Neural Networks and stochastic block models. The model will incorporate a sparsity-inducing prior and a budget constraint on node degree growth, inspired by physical limits in protein interaction and signaling. We will train and evaluate the model on large-scale single-cell perturbation benchmarks (e.g., Perturb-seq), using causal inference metrics to assess the fidelity of the inferred sublinear network structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new class of network models that explain sublinear densification, directly applicable to biological network inference from perturbation evidence. It will yield more parsimonious and physically plausible models of cellular regulation, advancing methods for interpreting high-throughput health data.
proposals.novelty: While most network science focuses on densifying graphs, we explicitly model sublinear growth driven by physical constraints, bridging Leskovec's work on network evolution with the specific challenge of building accurate models from single-cell evidence.

proposals.title: Direct Geometric Embedding: Learning Node Representations via Implicit Neighborhood Diffusion
proposals.motivation: Graph embedding methods like Node2Vec and DeepWalk rely on explicit random walks for neighborhood sampling, which introduces stochasticity, computational overhead, and sensitivity to hyperparameter tuning. In domains like single-cell biology and protein interaction networks, where graphs are constructed from noisy, high-dimensional data (e.g., perturbation responses or spatial transcriptomics), this stochastic sampling can obscure subtle but biologically critical functional relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that node embeddings can be learned more robustly and efficiently by directly optimizing a global objective that implicitly captures multi-hop neighborhood structure through a principled diffusion process, bypassing the need for explicit random walk sampling.
proposals.method: We will develop a method based on implicit neural representations and spectral graph theory. The core architecture will be a graph neural network (GNN) encoder trained to minimize a loss derived from the heat kernel—a continuous diffusion operator—which analytically encodes multi-scale neighborhood information. Specifically, we will use a simplified graph convolutional network (GCN) or GraphSAGE backbone to produce embeddings, and optimize them such that the dot product between node embeddings approximates the heat kernel matrix exp(-tL), where L is the normalized graph Laplacian and t is a diffusion time scale learned per node. This avoids explicit walk generation by using a closed-form, differentiable objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a deterministic, hyperparameter-robust alternative to sampling-based graph embedding, particularly valuable for inferring networks from single-cell data where signal is scarce. It will contribute a new, scalable framework for learning representations in biological and built environment networks that directly captures continuous diffusion of 'activity' or influence across cells or physical nodes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses random walks or matrix factorization, our approach uniquely integrates the analytic heat kernel into an end-to-end neural training loop, implicitly defining neighborhoods via continuous diffusion without sampling, which is novel for scalable graph representation learning.

proposals.title: Task-Optimized Graph Representation Learning for Single-Cell Perturbation Response Prediction
proposals.motivation: Inference of cellular networks from single-cell perturbation data is a core challenge in systems biology, with direct implications for understanding disease mechanisms and drug discovery. Current representation learning methods for cells and proteins often produce generic embeddings that are not optimized for the specific downstream task of predicting perturbation outcomes, limiting predictive accuracy and biological interpretability. This gap is critical, as precise prediction of cellular activity changes in response to genetic or chemical perturbations is essential for building causal models of cell function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that directly optimizing node (e.g., gene/protein) and cell embeddings for the specific task of predicting post-perturbation cell states will yield more accurate and generalizable models than using generic, task-agnostic embeddings. This task-dependent optimization will also produce representations that better capture the underlying physical and functional constraints of the cellular system.
proposals.method: We will develop a graph neural network (GNN) framework where the encoder is co-trained end-to-end with a task-specific prediction head. The input is a prior knowledge graph (built from pre-2026 protein-protein interaction and pathway databases) with nodes representing molecular entities. Single-cell RNA-seq profiles (pre- and post-perturbation) from large-scale benchmarks will be mapped onto this graph as node features. The model will be trained using a multi-task objective combining a supervised loss for predicting the perturbation response vector (e.g., changes in gene expression) and a self-supervised contrastive loss (inspired by GRPO and related methods) to ensure robust representation learning from stochastic single-cell data. The architecture will enforce calibration of prediction confidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, task-optimized framework for learning biologically meaningful representations from perturbation data, directly improving prediction accuracy for cellular response. It will contribute a new benchmark and evidence-based methodology for moving from generic network inference to task-driven reasoning in computational biology. The resulting embeddings and models will be a resource for hypothesis generation in health research.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard approaches that separate representation learning and task prediction, our method integrates them from the start, explicitly designing embeddings for a single, critical biological task. The novelty lies in the co-design of the graph-based representation and the perturbation prediction objective within a calibrated, stochastic modeling framework tailored for single-cell data.

proposals.title: Het2vec: A Flexible Framework for Embedding Heterogeneous Biological Networks via Meta-Path Guided Biased Random Walks
proposals.motivation: The success of node2vec in homogeneous networks has not fully translated to the complex, multi-typed networks prevalent in computational biology, such as those integrating protein-protein interactions, gene-disease associations, and single-cell activity data. Current heterogeneous graph embedding methods often require pre-defined meta-paths, limiting their adaptability and scalability for novel, large-scale biomedical knowledge graphs where the relevant semantic pathways are not known a priori. A generalized random walk framework that can automatically learn the importance of different node and edge types during walk sampling is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by parameterizing the node2vec bias parameters (p, q) as functions of node and edge types, and by incorporating a learnable type-transition matrix, we can generate walks that capture the rich, multi-relational structure of heterogeneous networks, leading to more informative embeddings for downstream tasks like protein function prediction or drug repurposing.
proposals.method: We will extend the node2vec algorithm by defining a type-aware transition probability. A learnable matrix will weight transitions between different node types via specific edge types, effectively learning a soft meta-path distribution. The traditional return (p) and in-out (q) parameters will be generalized to vectors conditioned on the node type of the previous, current, and candidate next nodes. We will train this model using a modified Skip-gram architecture that respects node type contexts, optimizing the walk biases and embeddings jointly via stochastic gradient descent on a heterogeneous network reconstruction objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, flexible framework for embedding heterogeneous networks without rigid meta-path specification, directly applicable to large-scale biological knowledge graphs. It will deliver a scalable open-source tool and demonstrate superior performance on benchmark tasks like link prediction in Hetionet and gene function inference from integrated single-cell and protein interaction data, advancing methods for multi-modal evidence synthesis in health.
proposals.novelty: Unlike meta-path-based models (e.g., metapath2vec) which fix walk schemas, our method learns the walk biases directly from data, and unlike simple heterogeneous extensions of DeepWalk, it explicitly models the complex, type-dependent interplay between the structural (BFS/DFS) and semantic (type compatibility) biases of random walks.

proposals.title: Attributed Network Representation Learning for Robust Inference of Single-Cell Perturbation Responses
proposals.motivation: Network inference from single-cell perturbation data is crucial for understanding cellular activity and protein interactions, but current methods often treat the network structure and node features (e.g., gene expression, protein levels) separately. This limits the ability to predict how perturbations propagate across a cell's built environment, as the rich, multi-modal evidence at each node is underutilized during feature learning. Integrating these complementary data sources is essential for more accurate and generalizable models of cellular health and disease mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a joint representation learning framework that explicitly combines graph topology with rich, high-dimensional node attributes will yield more robust and predictive models of single-cell perturbation outcomes than methods using either data type alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel graph neural network (GNN) architecture based on a message-passing framework (e.g., GraphSAGE or GAT) specifically adapted for single-cell perturbation graphs. The model will incorporate node features (e.g., baseline expression, protein abundance) directly into the aggregation and update functions. We will benchmark this approach against structure-only and feature-only baselines using the large-scale single-cell perturbation benchmark, employing evaluation metrics for causal effect prediction and network inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, principled method for fusing network structure with node-specific evidence in biological settings. It will deliver a software tool for the community and establish evidence on whether integrated feature learning improves the accuracy and calibration of predictions in stochastic biological systems, directly addressing themes of overconfidence in AI for science.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs are known, their tailored application to the specific challenge of causal inference from single-cell perturbation data, with a focus on preventing overconfident predictions for stochastic outcomes by leveraging multi-modal node data, is novel. This moves beyond retrieval of patterns to structured reasoning on attributed biological networks.

proposals.title: DyGRAE: Dynamic Graph Representation Learning for Evolving Cellular Activity Networks
proposals.motivation: Understanding cellular dynamics is crucial for interpreting perturbation effects and disease progression. Current single-cell methods infer static networks, but biological systems are inherently dynamic, with protein interactions and signaling pathways evolving over time. There is a critical need for computational frameworks that can learn continuous, low-dimensional representations of these evolving networks to model temporal processes like cell differentiation or drug response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamic graph representation learning model, trained on time-stamped single-cell perturbation data, can learn embeddings that capture the continuous evolution of cellular networks more accurately than discrete snapshot-based methods, leading to improved prediction of future cellular states.
proposals.method: We will develop DyGRAE (Dynamic Graph Recurrent Auto-Encoder), extending static graph neural networks like GraphSAGE or GATs. The model will integrate a continuous-time dynamic graph framework (inspired by CTDNE) with a recurrent or neural ODE-based encoder to update node embeddings as new perturbation evidence arrives. We will train the model on large-scale, time-course single-cell RNA-seq datasets post-perturbation, using a temporal link prediction and future state forecasting objective, benchmarked against discrete dynamic embedding baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for learning dynamic embeddings from evolving biological networks, directly applicable to time-course perturbation data. It will contribute a benchmark for evaluating temporal network inference in biology and offer new insights into the trajectory of cellular activity changes, with implications for understanding treatment dynamics and disease modeling.
proposals.novelty: While dynamic network embeddings exist for social networks, their application to continuous-time, evidence-driven biological networks from single-cell data is novel. The integration of neural ODEs for modeling the smooth evolution of protein interaction graphs represents a significant advance over discrete-time approaches.

proposals.title: Distill2Vec: Knowledge Distillation for Efficient and Transferable Node Embeddings in Biological Networks
proposals.motivation: Large-scale node2vec models are powerful for learning representations in biological networks (e.g., protein-protein interaction, single-cell perturbation graphs), but their computational cost limits deployment in resource-constrained settings like real-time health analytics or on-device applications. Pre-2026, knowledge distillation has succeeded in compressing large language and vision models, but its systematic application to graph embedding methods, particularly for preserving relational and functional semantics in biological data, remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: A smaller, distilled student model can be trained to mimic the embedding space of a large, pre-trained node2vec teacher model with minimal loss in downstream task performance (e.g., protein function prediction, cell type classification), while achieving significant gains in inference speed and memory efficiency.
proposals.method: We will first train a large, high-dimensional node2vec teacher model on heterogeneous biological networks (e.g., from STRING or single-cell perturbation benchmarks). Using a distillation loss (e.g., Mean Squared Error on output embeddings or a contrastive loss aligning student/teacher similarity structures), we will train a compact student model—a shallower neural network or a smaller embedding table. We will evaluate on held-out evidence from tasks like link prediction and node classification across built physical and activity networks, comparing to the teacher and baseline compression methods like PCA or quantization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for compressing graph embeddings, enabling faster inference for large-scale network analysis in computational biology. It will deliver empirical evidence on the trade-offs between size, speed, and accuracy for distilled embeddings, and release benchmark models for health-related network tasks.
proposals.novelty: While distillation is known, its targeted application to preserve the nuanced, multi-scale relational patterns captured by node2vec in biological contexts is novel. The approach integrates Leskovec's graph expertise with distillation methods to address efficiency in single-cell and protein network analysis, a critical gap pre-2026.

proposals.title: Dual-Representation Graph Networks: Learning Continuous Edge Features for Single-Cell Perturbation Analysis
proposals.motivation: Network inference from single-cell perturbation data is crucial for understanding cellular activity and protein interactions, but current graph neural network (GNN) methods primarily learn node embeddings, treating edges as static binary or simple weighted connections. This limits the ability to model complex, context-dependent relationships, such as how a drug's effect (an edge) varies across different cell states or physical microenvironments. A framework that learns rich, continuous representations for edges directly from data could provide more accurate and interpretable models of biological systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a graph neural architecture that jointly learns continuous feature vectors for both nodes and edges will significantly improve the accuracy of inferring causal interaction networks from single-cell perturbation data, compared to methods that use fixed or scalar edge attributes.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the Graph Network (GN) block framework (Battaglia et al., 2018) to create a Dual-Representation Graph Network (Dual-GN). The core innovation is an edge-update function that, like the node-update function, uses a multilayer perceptron (MLP) to transform concatenated features from the edge itself, its incident nodes, and the global graph context into a new continuous edge representation. We will implement this using PyTorch Geometric and train it on large-scale single-cell perturbation benchmarks (e.g., Perturb-seq) with a link prediction objective, using evidence from protein-protein interaction databases for supervision.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, general-purpose GNN architecture for learning dual (node and edge) representations, validated in the critical domain of biological network inference. It will provide a new methodological tool for modeling complex relational data where interactions have rich, learnable attributes, advancing beyond static adjacency matrices. The resulting models could offer more nuanced insights into how built perturbation programs affect cellular activity networks.
proposals.novelty: While some GNNs propagate information across edges, they rarely treat the edge features as first-class, learnable, continuous latent vectors that are dynamically updated alongside nodes. Our method explicitly dualizes the representation learning process, making the edge representation a central, adaptive component of the model, which is non-obvious in standard node-centric frameworks.

proposals.title: Subgraph-Centric Learning for Scalable and Generalizable Network Inference in Biological Systems
proposals.motivation: Modern network inference in biology, such as reconstructing protein-protein interaction or gene regulatory networks from single-cell perturbation data, requires training on massive, ever-growing graphs. This full-graph training paradigm is computationally prohibitive and often infeasible for dynamic, real-world systems like those modeling cellular activity across tissues. A method that learns effectively from small, biased subgraphs would dramatically increase the scalability and applicability of graph machine learning in health and systems biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained strategically on a small set of informative, biased subgraphs—such as those centered on perturbed nodes in a biological network—can achieve comparable or superior generalization performance to a model trained on the entire graph, by focusing learning on causally relevant local structures.
proposals.method: We will develop a subgraph-centric learning framework that integrates ideas from graph subsampling, meta-learning, and causal inference. The method will actively sample biased subgraphs based on node-level features (e.g., protein expression from single-cell data) and evidence of perturbation, rather than random walks. We will employ a GNN architecture with a readout function designed for subgraph-level representations, trained using a contrastive or meta-learning objective (e.g., MAML) to ensure representations generalize from the biased subgraphs to the full, unseen network structure. Performance will be benchmarked on established single-cell perturbation datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, scalable framework for network inference and prediction in massive biological graphs, directly applicable to problems in health and built molecular networks. It will contribute a theoretical and empirical understanding of the conditions under which subgraph-based training is sufficient, challenging the prevailing need for full-graph access.
proposals.novelty: While graph sampling methods exist, our approach uniquely biases subgraph selection based on experimental perturbation evidence and combines it with meta-learning for generalization, moving beyond simple scalability to learning transferable causal patterns from limited, biased observations.

proposals.title: Temporal Graph Dynamics on Biological Networks: Learning Evolving Structure and Function from Static OGB Benchmarks
proposals.motivation: Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) datasets like `ogbl-ppa` and `ogbl-biokg` model biological interactions as static snapshots, ignoring the fundamental temporal dynamics of cellular processes, protein-protein interactions, and disease progression. This static assumption limits the ability of graph neural networks (GNNs) to model causal relationships and predict system evolution, which is critical for applications in computational biology and health. A method to infer and learn from the latent temporal dynamics underlying these static graphs would bridge a significant gap between benchmark modeling and real-world biological systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by treating static OGB biological graphs as observations from a latent, continuous-time dynamic process, we can learn a model that infers plausible temporal evolution, leading to improved performance on tasks like link prediction, robustness analysis, and the generation of biologically realistic perturbation trajectories.
proposals.method: We will develop a neural temporal point process framework where each node and edge in an OGB graph is associated with a latent, continuously evolving representation. Building on pre-2026 methods like TGAT and DyRep, we will use attention-based mechanisms to model how node features and adjacency structures co-eolve. The model will be trained via a self-supervised objective that reconstructs the final static graph from stochastically sampled earlier temporal 'slices', regularized by evidence from known biological sequence and interaction kinetics (e.g., protein turnover rates). We will evaluate on temporally split OGB data and novel tasks predicting system response to in-silico perturbations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new benchmark paradigm for evaluating GNNs on inferred temporal dynamics, 2) a generative model that can propose evolution trajectories for biological networks, providing hypotheses for wet-lab validation, and 3) evidence on whether modeling latent dynamics improves generalization and robustness over static GNNs on canonical tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the standard static graph learning problem: instead of learning from observed temporal sequences, we learn to infer the latent dynamics that explain a single static observation, which is non-trivial and underexplored, especially for large-scale biological knowledge graphs.

proposals.title: A Universal Graph Foundation Model for the Open Graph Benchmark: Challenging Task-Specific Design
proposals.motivation: The field of graph machine learning is fragmented, with models typically designed and tuned for specific tasks (e.g., node classification, link prediction) or domains (e.g., biology, social networks). The Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) provides a diverse, large-scale testbed for this fragmentation, but current top-performing solutions are highly specialized. This limits practical utility and general scientific understanding, as it remains unclear what core principles enable robust performance across the full spectrum of graph-structured data.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, universal graph model architecture, trained with a multi-task objective spanning diverse OGB datasets, can achieve competitive or superior performance to specialized state-of-the-art models across all OGB tasks, demonstrating that task- and domain-specific inductive biases can be learned rather than hand-engineered.
proposals.method: We will develop a transformer-based graph model with a flexible, modality-agnostic input encoder to handle diverse node/edge features (e.g., protein sequences, citation text, physical coordinates). Training will employ a two-stage strategy: first, large-scale self-supervised pre-training on a unified corpus of graphs from OGB and related sources (e.g., biomolecular networks, built environment graphs) using a combination of masked component prediction and contrastive objectives. Second, we will perform parameter-efficient multi-task fine-tuning on the full suite of OGB tasks using adapters or prompt tuning, rigorously evaluating on the official OGB leaderboards against task-specific baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first rigorously evaluated universal graph foundation model, setting a new benchmark for generalization in graph ML. It will provide evidence for or against the feasibility of a 'one model for all graphs' paradigm, with significant implications for scalable and practical graph AI in health (protein function) and other domains. The model and training framework will be released as a new baseline for the community.
proposals.novelty: While graph foundation models are an emerging concept pre-2026, none have been systematically designed and evaluated to outperform specialized models across the entire, standardized OGB suite, which includes fundamentally different task types (node, link, graph) and domains (biological, social, physical). The proposal directly challenges the necessity of task-specific architecture design.

proposals.title: Efficient Graph Transformers: Scaling Long-Range Interaction Modeling on Large-Scale Biological Networks
proposals.motivation: Modeling long-range dependencies in large-scale graphs, such as protein-protein interaction or cell signaling networks from OGB, is crucial for tasks like protein function prediction or drug response inference. Standard Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) suffer from over-squashing and limited receptive fields, while full self-attention on graphs scales quadratically with node count, making it infeasible for massive networks. Pre-2026 advances in efficient Transformers (e.g., Linformer, Performer, BigBird) in NLP offer a promising but underexplored path for graphs.
proposals.hypothesis: Sparse or linear-complexity Transformer architectures, adapted to respect graph structure, can effectively capture long-range interactions in large-scale biological graphs, leading to superior performance on prediction tasks compared to message-passing GNNs and standard Graph Transformers.
proposals.method: We will adapt and rigorously benchmark pre-2026 efficient Transformer architectures (specifically Performer for linear attention and BigBird for sparse, global attention) for graph-structured data. The core adaptation involves integrating graph inductive biases: using graph diffusion kernels (e.g., Personalized PageRank) to sparsify or guide attention patterns, and employing Laplacian positional encodings. We will implement these models within the OGB framework and evaluate them on large-scale benchmarks like ogbl-ppa and ogbl-collab, comparing against state-of-the-art GNNs and full Graph Transformers on accuracy, scalability, and long-range dependency capture.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, open-source framework for efficient Graph Transformers, validated on canonical biological network benchmarks. It will provide systematic evidence on which long-range modeling strategies are most effective for large graphs, advancing methods for network inference from single-cell and protein interaction data. The findings will directly inform the design of next-generation models for health and systems biology applications.
proposals.novelty: While Graph Transformers exist, their application has been limited to smaller graphs or dense attention. The novelty lies in the systematic adaptation and evaluation of *provably efficient* Transformer variants from NLP to massive, real-world biological graphs, explicitly tackling the scalability bottleneck to unlock long-range reasoning.

proposals.title: GraphSSL: Self-Supervised Pretext Tasks for Learning Robust Representations of Cellular Activity Networks
proposals.motivation: Inferring networks from single-cell perturbation data is crucial for understanding cellular activity and protein interactions, but current methods often rely on limited, noisy evidence. Self-supervised learning (SSL) has revolutionized representation learning in vision and NLP by creating rich, transferable features from unlabeled data. However, graph-specific pretext tasks for biological networks, particularly those capturing physical and built environment influences on cell health, remain underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that designing graph-specific self-supervised pretext tasks, inspired by contrastive and generative SSL, will yield more robust, generalizable, and information-rich representations of cellular interaction networks than supervised approaches alone.
proposals.method: We will design a suite of graph pretext tasks for single-cell network inference. This includes a contrastive task using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with node/edge masking and subgraph sampling to learn invariant representations. A generative task will employ a graph autoencoder to reconstruct perturbed network structures or predict masked protein interaction edges. We will benchmark these methods on large-scale single-cell perturbation datasets, evaluating their ability to improve downstream tasks like link prediction and classification of network phenotypes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable framework for learning from biological network data with limited labels. It will deliver a set of validated, graph-specific SSL tasks and pre-trained models that can enhance inference accuracy and robustness in computational biology, directly advancing methods for health-related network analysis.
proposals.novelty: While SSL is established in vision/NLP, its adaptation to graphs for biological inference is non-trivial. The novelty lies in tailoring pretext tasks to the unique structure of perturbation-derived networks (e.g., capturing causal 'activity across cell' relationships) rather than applying generic graph SSL methods.

proposals.title: Synthetic Micro-Graphs: A Benchmark Suite for Scalability and Generalization in Graph Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Real-world graphs in domains like molecular biology and social networks are massive, making systematic evaluation of graph algorithms' scalability and generalization properties slow and expensive. Existing synthetic benchmarks often fail to capture the intricate structural and functional properties that create these challenges, leading to a gap between synthetic performance and real-world utility. A principled method to generate small, hard graphs that distill these core challenges is needed for rapid iteration and robust model development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling and instantiating key structural motifs (e.g., hierarchical communities, long-range dependencies, heterogeneous node roles) and functional constraints (e.g., multi-task node labels, perturbation resilience) observed in large-scale biological and social networks, we can create micro-graphs that reliably reproduce the failure modes of state-of-the-art graph neural networks (GNNs) on massive real-world tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a generative framework where graphs are constructed via a multi-stage process. First, we define a library of 'challenge modules' inspired by pre-2026 analysis of real networks (e.g., protein-protein interaction hierarchies, social echo chambers). Each module encodes a specific hardness dimension like over-squashing or poor cross-graph generalization. We will then combine these modules using constrained graph operations to create tiny synthetic graphs (<100 nodes). The hardness will be validated by testing the performance gap between simple GNN baselines (e.g., GCN, GAT) and more sophisticated architectures (e.g., Graph Transformers, methods with explicit long-range propagation) on downstream tasks like node classification and graph property prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) A publicly available benchmark suite of 'Micro-Graphs' for fast, diagnostic evaluation of graph learning methods. 2) A taxonomy of graph structural properties that induce specific scalability and generalization failures. 3) Evidence-based guidelines for architecture design to address these identified failure modes, directly benefiting fields like single-cell network inference and drug discovery where graph data is paramount.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior synthetic graph generators focused on statistical fidelity or scalability *up*, our method is the first to focus on scaling *down* while preserving algorithmic hardness, creating a diagnostic tool rather than a data substitute. It uniquely bridges the 'activity' and 'physical' constraints of real systems (e.g., cell, protein networks) with the 'methods' development cycle in graph ML.

proposals.title: Inverse Graph Generation: Counterfactual Graph Synthesis for Interpreting and Controlling GNN Predictions on Biological Networks
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are powerful for tasks like protein function prediction (OGB), but they are black boxes. Understanding what graph structures drive a model's prediction is crucial for scientific discovery, especially in biology where the network (e.g., protein-protein interaction) is often a hypothesis itself. Current methods explain predictions by identifying important subgraphs but cannot generate novel, plausible graph edits that would systematically alter the model's output.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generative model, conditioned on a trained GNN's predictions, can learn to synthesize minimal, biologically plausible counterfactual graph modifications that flip the model's prediction, thereby revealing the model's causal reasoning and suggesting testable biological interventions.
proposals.method: We will frame this as an inverse optimization problem. Using a pre-trained GNN (e.g., on OGB-Protein) as a fixed black-box function, we will train a conditional graph variational autoencoder (C-VGAE) or a graph diffusion model. The generator will be conditioned on the target node's initial prediction and a desired prediction delta. The objective will combine an adversarial loss (to match the target prediction change) with a realism penalty using a pre-trained graph autoencoder on the original biological network data (e.g., STRING) and domain constraints (e.g., protein family compatibility). We will adapt GRPO-style policy optimization to handle the discrete, stochastic graph generation process.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel tool for interpretable AI in biology, moving from subgraph identification to actionable graph hypothesis generation. It will contribute a benchmark for counterfactual graph explanation and a method to propose targeted network perturbations (e.g., inferred missing interactions) that could be validated experimentally.
proposals.novelty: While graph generation and explanation exist, inverting a fixed model's predictions to generate minimal, domain-grounded counterfactual edits is unexplored. This bridges model interpretation with experimental design in systems biology.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Node Features: Understanding the Limits of Graph Neural Networks in Ultra-High-Dimensional Feature Regimes
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have advanced significantly, but research has focused on scaling graph structure (e.g., large-scale benchmarks for network inference) or improving reasoning. The scaling of node features, particularly in domains like single-cell biology where features (e.g., protein expression, gene activity) are extremely high-dimensional but graphs (e.g., cell-cell interactions) are moderate-sized, is poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to build predictive models from rich, multi-modal evidence in health and biological networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in the regime of extremely high-dimensional node features on moderate-sized graphs, standard GNN architectures will fail due to feature overparameterization and noise amplification, but novel regularization and feature distillation methods can recover robust performance.
proposals.method: We will systematically benchmark GNNs (e.g., GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE) and graph transformers on synthetic and real-world datasets (e.g., single-cell perturbation data with protein/activity features) while scaling node feature dimensions from 10^3 to 10^5+. We will introduce and evaluate novel techniques: 1) structured sparsity via biological pathway priors for feature selection, 2) decoupled feature autoencoders for dimensionality reduction before graph propagation, and 3) gradient-based attribution methods to diagnose overconfidence, inspired by analysis of uncalibrated reasoning in stochastic outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of GNN scaling limits with high-dimensional features, yielding practical guidelines for model design in computational biology and health. It will contribute new benchmark datasets and a suite of methods for robust feature integration, directly advancing the construction of reliable models from single-cell and other rich feature sources.
proposals.novelty: While scaling graph structure is well-studied, the systematic investigation of ultra-high-dimensional node feature scaling is novel. The approach uniquely combines insights from biological network inference with modern analysis of model overconfidence and feature attribution.

proposals.title: DUAL: Data-Efficient and Unsupervised Graph Learning via Sparsification for Biological Network Compression
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are central to modeling complex biological systems, from protein-protein interaction networks to single-cell perturbation graphs. However, large-scale graphs like those in the Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) are computationally expensive, limiting deployment in resource-constrained settings like real-time health analytics. Pre-2026, model compression focuses on pruning neural network weights, but principled methods for compressing the underlying graph structure itself—critical for biological data—remain underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that biological and molecular interaction graphs contain significant structural redundancy, and that systematic, task-aware sparsification can identify minimal subgraphs that preserve, or even enhance, the performance and robustness of GNNs on downstream tasks like protein function prediction.
proposals.method: We will develop DUAL, a framework that applies and evaluates multiple sparsification strategies (e.g., spectral sparsification, influence-based edge scoring, and gradient-guided node pruning) to OGB biological graphs (e.g., ogbl-ppa, ogbl-ddi). We will integrate these methods with GNN architectures common in Leskovec's recent work (e.g., GraphSAGE, GAT) and measure performance on established tasks. The core innovation is an unsupervised, task-agnostic sparsification objective that minimizes the spectral distortion of the graph Laplacian, followed by a fine-tuning phase to recover task-specific performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a systematic benchmark for graph sparsification methods on real-world biological networks, 2) practical algorithms for creating compact, performant graph representations that reduce computational overhead for inference, and 3) new evidence on the intrinsic compressibility of biological relational data, informing more efficient model design.
proposals.novelty: While network pruning is well-studied in deep learning, applying it directly to the input graph structure of GNNs—especially for biological data where edges represent physical interactions or evidence-based relationships—is a distinct and non-trivial problem. Our approach uniquely combines spectral graph theory with modern GNN training to achieve compression without requiring extensive task-specific labels during sparsification.

proposals.title: Benchmarking the Limits of Graph Neural Networks: A Task-Complexity Hierarchy on Fixed-Scale OGB Datasets
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are widely benchmarked on tasks like node classification, but performance is often conflated with dataset scale. A critical, underexplored question is how GNN architectures perform as task complexity increases on a fixed graph structure, isolating the challenge of long-range reasoning from scalability. This is directly relevant to modeling complex systems like protein-protein interaction networks or cellular signaling pathways, where accurate multi-hop inference is crucial for tasks like drug target identification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that on fixed-scale graphs, the performance of standard GNN architectures (e.g., GCN, GAT) will degrade predictably with increased task complexity (e.g., longer-range dependencies), and that novel architectures incorporating explicit multi-hop reasoning or latent variable models will demonstrate superior robustness to this complexity.
proposals.method: We will define a hierarchy of tasks on established Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) datasets (e.g., ogbn-proteins, ogbn-arxiv). Starting from standard node classification, we will design progressively harder tasks, such as predicting the properties of k-hop neighborhood aggregates, inferring missing edges in multi-hop paths, or classifying nodes based on distant, perturbed subgraphs. We will benchmark standard GNNs, graph transformers, and methods from Leskovec's recent work on network inference from single-cell perturbation data, adapting their causal reasoning frameworks. Evaluation will focus on the performance drop-off curve relative to task complexity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, fine-grained benchmarking suite that decouples task complexity from graph scale, offering clearer diagnostic power for GNN architectures. It will produce concrete evidence on the long-range reasoning limitations of current methods and may inspire new architectures tailored for complex relational reasoning, with implications for computational biology and knowledge graph completion.
proposals.novelty: While OGB evaluates scale, no benchmark systematically increases *reasoning complexity* on fixed graphs. The novelty lies in the controlled experimental design that isolates the challenge of multi-hop dependency learning, creating a direct testbed for methods advancing from retrieval to reasoning on graph-structured data.

proposals.title: Full-Graph Inductive Learning via Node-Specific Parameterization and Adaptive Sparsification
proposals.motivation: Mini-batch training via neighborhood sampling has become a computational necessity for scaling Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to large graphs, but it introduces significant complexity, variance, and can obscure true global dependencies. This is particularly critical in domains like single-cell biology and health, where inferring accurate, evidence-based protein interaction or perturbation-response networks from single-cell data requires stable and holistic gradient signals. A method that enables efficient full-graph inductive training would simplify the training pipeline and potentially improve model fidelity for downstream reasoning tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by decoupling node representation updates from the explicit propagation of full-graph Laplacians through node-specific parameterization and adaptive graph sparsification, we can train GNNs inductively on entire graphs without mini-batches, avoiding the pitfalls of sampling while remaining computationally tractable.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel architecture where each node has a small set of adaptive, local parameters, initialized from a shared meta-network, allowing for node-specific computation without global message passing in every forward pass. The model will employ an adaptive spectral sparsification technique, dynamically maintaining a sparse, data-dependent approximation of the graph Laplacian to control computational cost. Training will proceed via full-graph forward passes with this sparse structure, using gradient checkpointing and efficient kernel operations to manage memory, directly optimizing for tasks like network inference from single-cell perturbation data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, simplified paradigm for scalable GNN training, reducing system complexity and potentially offering more stable convergence than stochastic sampling methods. It will deliver a practical tool for biomedical network inference, where full-graph context is valuable, and contribute a theoretical framework for understanding the necessity of mini-batching in graph representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While existing methods focus on better samplers or subgraph selection, our approach challenges the core need for any sampling in the forward pass by combining node-specific models with adaptive graph sparsification, a direction largely unexplored in pre-2026 GNN literature.

proposals.title: Directed and Heterogeneous GraphSAGE: A General Framework for Representation Learning on Complex Biological Networks
proposals.motivation: GraphSAGE has become a cornerstone for learning on undirected, homogeneous graphs, but many real-world biological systems are inherently more complex. Single-cell perturbation data, protein-protein interaction networks, and knowledge graphs linking genes, diseases, and drugs are characterized by directed relationships and heterogeneous node/edge types. Existing methods often simplify these structures, losing critical biological information about causality, interaction directionality, and entity diversity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the inductive GraphSAGE framework to explicitly model edge direction and node/edge heterogeneity will yield significantly more informative embeddings, leading to improved performance on downstream tasks like protein function prediction, perturbation response inference, and drug repurposing.
proposals.method: We will develop a new framework, DH-GraphSAGE, with two core modules. For directed graphs, we will implement a dual-neighborhood sampler that aggregates separately from in-coming and out-going neighbors, combining them via a learnable directional attention mechanism. For heterogeneity, we will design type-specific aggregation functions, where the parameters of the aggregator (e.g., a Gated Recurrent Unit) are conditioned on the edge type or the neighbor node type, building on pre-2026 relational GCN ideas but within an inductive, sampling-based paradigm. The model will be evaluated on benchmark datasets from single-cell network inference and biomedical knowledge graphs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, scalable, and inductive method for learning on complex biological graphs, directly applicable to the researcher's recent work on single-cell perturbation benchmarks and protein networks. It will advance the field by moving graph representation learning beyond structural simplicity to capture the nuanced, typed, and causal relationships fundamental to biology and health.
proposals.novelty: While relational GCNs and directed GATs exist, the novelty lies in integrating these capabilities into the highly practical and scalable GraphSAGE sampling framework, creating a first-of-its-kind inductive model that jointly handles directionality and heterogeneity without requiring the full graph during training.

proposals.title: Edge-Aware Graph Neural Networks for Multi-Relational Biological Network Inference from Single-Cell Data
proposals.motivation: Current graph neural networks (GNNs) for biological network inference, such as those applied to single-cell perturbation data, primarily aggregate information based on node features and adjacency, treating edges as binary or uniformly weighted connections. This is a significant limitation in biological systems where interactions (edges) are multi-faceted, carrying rich, typed information such as protein-protein interaction strength, regulatory effect direction, or causal confidence derived from perturbation evidence. Ignoring these edge features limits the model's capacity to learn accurate, interpretable representations of complex cellular activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly incorporating multi-dimensional edge features into the message-passing framework of GNNs will significantly improve their accuracy and robustness in inferring gene regulatory and protein interaction networks from single-cell perturbation data, leading to more biologically plausible and predictive models.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel Edge-Aware Graph Neural Network (EA-GNN) architecture that generalizes standard aggregation functions (e.g., GCN, GAT). The core innovation is a parameterized message function, m_uv = φ(h_u, h_v, e_uv), where e_uv is a feature vector for the edge (u,v), and φ is a small neural network (e.g., MLP). This allows the model to dynamically weight and transform messages based on relational context. We will benchmark this architecture against state-of-the-art methods on established single-cell perturbation datasets, evaluating performance on link prediction and network recovery tasks. The model will be designed to handle both continuous (e.g., interaction score) and categorical (e.g., interaction type) edge features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a flexible, general-purpose framework for incorporating edge features into GNN-based biological network inference, moving beyond simple adjacency. It will deliver a new benchmark model for the community and release open-source code. The resulting inferred networks are expected to have higher biological fidelity, offering better substrates for downstream tasks like drug target identification or understanding cellular response mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While edge-feature-aware GNNs exist in other domains (e.g., knowledge graphs), their systematic application and evaluation for the specific challenge of *inferring* biological networks from noisy, high-dimensional single-cell data is novel. This approach directly integrates the 'evidence' and 'protein' interaction context from recent methods into the core learning machinery, rather than treating it as a post-hoc filter.

proposals.title: Inductive Representation Learning for Real-Time Streaming Biological Networks
proposals.motivation: Modern biological systems, from protein-protein interaction networks to single-cell perturbation responses, are dynamic, with entities and relationships evolving continuously. Pre-2026 graph neural network (GNN) methods, including those from Leskovec's lab, excel at inductive learning on static graphs but struggle with the streaming setting where nodes and edges arrive in real-time. This limits their application to critical, time-sensitive domains like monitoring cellular signaling cascades or real-time health alerts from built environment sensor networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an inductive GNN framework, augmented with a temporal memory module and calibrated streaming inference, can learn stable, generalizable node and graph representations from unbounded, high-velocity biological and health interaction streams.
proposals.method: We will adapt the GraphSAGE inductive framework to operate on continuous-time dynamic graphs. The core architecture will integrate a recurrent memory bank (inspired by TGN) to capture temporal node histories, coupled with a streaming subgraph sampling strategy to bound computational cost. To address overconfidence in stochastic predictions—a known issue from recent uncalibrated reasoning research—we will incorporate an online Platt scaling layer for confidence calibration. The system will be evaluated on large-scale, timestamped single-cell perturbation cascades and real-world health monitoring graphs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, principled method for inductive learning on never-ending biological and health interaction graphs, moving beyond static snapshots. It will deliver a benchmarked framework and new evidence on the interplay between temporal dynamics, generalization, and predictive uncertainty in streaming graph AI.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing the inductive power of frameworks like GraphSAGE with the demands of unending, high-throughput biological data streams, while explicitly tackling the challenge of calibrated, overconfident predictions in this dynamic, stochastic environment—a combination not addressed by pre-2026 temporal or static GNNs.

proposals.title: Meta-Graph: Meta-Learning Initializations for Fast Adaptation of Graph Neural Networks to Single-Cell Perturbation Networks
proposals.motivation: Inferring gene regulatory or protein-protein interaction networks from single-cell perturbation data is a core challenge in computational biology, but models must generalize across diverse cell types and experimental conditions. Current GNNs require extensive retraining for each new graph, which is computationally prohibitive and data-inefficient for large-scale biological discovery. A method for rapid adaptation to new cellular contexts would accelerate the analysis of perturbation atlases and the identification of context-specific interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, specifically Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML), can learn a set of initial parameters for a GNN aggregator function that enables fast, few-shot adaptation to new single-cell-derived graphs with minimal fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will frame each single-cell perturbation experiment (e.g., from a benchmark like the one in Leskovec's recent work) as a separate task for meta-learning. Using MAML, we will train a shared GNN encoder (e.g., a Graph Attention Network) on a distribution of such tasks to find initial parameters that are sensitive to gradient-based updates. The inner-loop optimization will perform a few steps of fine-tuning on a held-out graph's support set, and the outer loop will update the meta-initialization to minimize loss on the query set across tasks. Performance will be evaluated on unseen perturbation graphs, measuring the speed and final accuracy of link prediction or node classification after adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical framework for rapid GNN adaptation in biological network inference, directly applicable to large-scale single-cell data. It will provide empirical evidence on the transferability of learned structural priors across cellular contexts and establish a new benchmark for few-shot learning on biological graphs.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been applied to few-shot image classification, its application to rapidly adapt GNN aggregators for inference on *new graph topologies* derived from single-cell data is novel. This bridges Leskovec's expertise in activity-based network inference with advanced, pre-2026 meta-learning methods for physical and biological systems.

proposals.title: MicroGraphSAGE: Data-Efficient and Generalizable Graph Representation Learning for Few-Shot Biological Networks
proposals.motivation: Graph neural networks (GNNs) like GraphSAGE excel on large-scale networks but often fail on tiny graphs or with extremely sparse labels, which are common in critical biomedical domains like single-cell perturbation analysis and protein-protein interaction prediction. Current methods lack principled approaches to prevent overfitting and leverage relational inductive biases effectively in these data-scarce, high-stakes settings, limiting their utility for scientific discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly regularizing the aggregation and transformation steps of GraphSAGE and incorporating meta-learning principles, we can create a model that generalizes robustly from micro-graphs and few-shot node labels, outperforming standard GNNs and naive fine-tuning approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop MicroGraphSAGE, extending the GraphSAGE framework with three key modifications: 1) a learnable, sparsity-inducing attention mechanism over neighbor aggregators to prevent over-smoothing on small graphs, 2) a contrastive self-supervised pre-training objective using graph augmentations suitable for biological networks (e.g., edge dropout, feature masking) to learn transferable representations, and 3) a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) outer loop to optimize the model for rapid adaptation to new few-shot tasks, explicitly balancing task-specific learning with prior structural knowledge.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, scalable framework for graph learning in low-data biological regimes, directly applicable to inferring networks from single-cell data or predicting protein function with limited evidence. We will contribute a new benchmark suite of micro-graph datasets from biological domains and establish best practices for data-efficient GNN design.
proposals.novelty: While few-shot learning exists for images and text, its principled integration with GNNs for micro-scale graphs is underexplored. Our approach uniquely combines intrinsic GNN architectural regularization for small graphs with extrinsic meta-learning optimization, moving beyond simple pre-training and fine-tuning.

proposals.title: Graph Compression via Inductive Embeddings: A Framework for Efficient Storage and Retrieval of Large-Scale Biological Networks
proposals.motivation: The explosion of single-cell and protein-protein interaction data has led to massive, high-dimensional biological graphs that are costly to store, share, and query. Traditional graph compression methods often sacrifice the relational structure needed for downstream inference tasks. A method that compresses graphs into compact, task-aware embeddings would enable efficient data management while preserving utility for analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inductive graph neural network (GNN) embeddings, trained to reconstruct key structural and functional properties, can serve as an effective lossy compression scheme, where the compressed representation (the model and its latent vectors) is significantly smaller than the original graph yet retains sufficient information for accurate downstream prediction tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a graph is compressed by training an inductive GNN encoder (e.g., GraphSAGE or a GAT) to generate node embeddings. The compression loss will combine a reconstruction term (via a decoder) with task-specific terms (e.g., for protein function or cell type classification) to preserve semantically important information. The 'compressed' storage consists of the fixed encoder model parameters and the final layer's embedding matrix. We will benchmark compression ratio and fidelity against baselines like graph summarization and spectral methods on large-scale single-cell perturbation and protein interaction networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, task-aware paradigm for graph compression grounded in representation learning, 2) a systematic evaluation of the trade-off between compression ratio and predictive fidelity on real-world biological networks, and 3) an open-source tool for compressing large graphs in domains like health and systems biology, where data volume is a growing barrier.
proposals.novelty: While graph embeddings are widely used for prediction, framing the embedding process explicitly as a lossy compression scheme—optimizing the storage cost of the model plus embeddings against reconstruction of network properties—is a novel and underexplored dual problem. This connects representation learning directly to data engineering challenges in science.

proposals.title: Inverse Graph Embedding: Reconstructing Biological Networks from Learned Single-Cell Representations
proposals.motivation: Network inference from single-cell perturbation data is a core challenge in systems biology, but current methods rely on observed or assumed graph structures to learn meaningful embeddings. This limits discovery in domains like protein interaction or cell signaling, where the true underlying network is unknown. Inverting this paradigm—generating graph structure from learned embeddings—could enable de novo reconstruction of biological mechanisms directly from high-dimensional activity data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relational structure implicit in learned, task-specific embeddings of single-cell perturbation states contains sufficient information to reconstruct a biologically plausible and predictive interaction graph. This reconstructed graph will capture causal or regulatory relationships not present in prior knowledge networks.
proposals.method: We will develop a generative framework where a Graph Neural Network (GNN) encoder first produces embeddings from single-cell perturbation profiles (e.g., transcriptomic or proteomic). A separate, invertible decoder module—inspired by techniques from generative adversarial networks or normalizing flows—will then map pairs of embeddings to probabilistic edge predictions. We will benchmark this on large-scale single-cell perturbation datasets, using held-out genetic interactions and known pathway evidence for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, end-to-end method for data-driven biological network inference that does not require a predefined graph. It will contribute a benchmark for evaluating the fidelity of graph reconstruction from embeddings and offer new insights into the interpretability of learned representations in computational biology.
proposals.novelty: While graph representation learning is well-established, systematically inverting the process to generate a discrete, interpretable graph from embeddings is non-trivial and underexplored, especially for single-cell data. This moves beyond using embeddings for downstream tasks to explicitly recover the latent relational model itself.

proposals.title: Inferring Node Features for Graph Neural Networks: A Self-Supervised Framework for Sparse and Missing Cellular Data
proposals.motivation: In single-cell biology and health-related network analysis, many nodes (e.g., rare cell types, understudied proteins) have entirely missing or extremely sparse feature vectors, severely limiting the performance of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for tasks like perturbation response prediction or protein function inference. Current imputation or zero-filling methods are inadequate, as they fail to leverage the graph structure and activity patterns across nodes to generate meaningful, task-specific representations. This feature incompleteness is a major bottleneck for building predictive models from large-scale, noisy biological networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-supervised learning framework, which jointly learns to reconstruct node features from network context and neighbor activity while optimizing for downstream tasks, can generate robust, informative embeddings for nodes with missing features that outperform generic imputation.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-task GNN architecture. The primary task is a downstream objective (e.g., classifying perturbation effects). A secondary, self-supervised head will perform feature reconstruction via a masked autoencoder, where known node features are randomly masked during training. For nodes with truly missing features, we will initialize with a learnable prior vector. The model will use a relational graph attention (GAT) mechanism to weight evidence from neighboring nodes with varying feature completeness, explicitly modeling uncertainty in the inferred features. Training will use a loss combining the downstream task loss and the reconstruction loss, balanced via gradient surgery.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, generalizable method for handling pervasive feature sparsity in biological and health knowledge graphs. It will deliver a benchmark and evaluation protocol for the 'feature-complete node embedding' problem, advancing the practical application of GNNs in domains like single-cell genomics and protein-protein interaction networks where data is inherently incomplete.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard graph imputation or simple aggregation, our method integrates feature inference directly into the GNN's optimization for a specific predictive task, creating embeddings that are both structurally informed and functionally relevant. The explicit modeling of neighbor reliability and feature uncertainty for sparse nodes is a novel extension of pre-2026 graph representation learning techniques.

proposals.title: Scalable Heterogeneous Graph Convolutional Networks for Multi-Modal Biological Systems
