proposals.motivation: Real-world biological systems, such as protein-protein interaction networks with drug perturbations or cellular signaling pathways, are inherently heterogeneous, involving multiple node types (e.g., genes, proteins, cells, compounds). Pre-2026 Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) frameworks, while powerful for scalability, are primarily designed for homogeneous graphs. Applying them directly to heterogeneous graphs by ignoring node type information loses critical relational semantics, limiting their utility for tasks like multi-modal drug response prediction or inferring cell-type-specific signaling from single-cell perturbation data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the scalable, sampling-based GCN framework (e.g., GraphSAGE, PinSage) to explicitly model multiple node and edge types will yield more accurate and interpretable representations for heterogeneous biological networks, significantly improving predictive performance on tasks like protein function annotation and perturbation outcome prediction.
proposals.method: We will develop HetSAGE, a heterogeneous extension of the GraphSAGE/PinSage architecture. The core innovation is a type-aware neighbor sampler and aggregator. For each target node, we will perform stratified sampling across its different relation types (e.g., protein-interacts-with-protein, drug-targets-protein). Separate, relation-specific aggregator functions (e.g., MLPs) will transform neighbor features before a type-specific pooling operation. We will benchmark on pre-2026 datasets like Hetionet and single-cell perturbation graphs, framing tasks as node classification (e.g., gene disease association) and link prediction (e.g., drug-target interaction).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a scalable, open-source framework for heterogeneous graph learning that maintains the efficiency of sampling-based GCNs, 2) rigorous evidence of its superiority over homogeneous and meta-path-based approaches on biological prediction tasks, and 3) demonstrated applications in translating multi-modal network data (genes, proteins, cells, compounds) into actionable health insights.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating the scalable inductive sampling paradigm of PinSage—designed for web-scale homogeneous graphs—with the semantic modeling of heterogeneous graph neural networks (RGCNs), an underexplored synthesis pre-2026, specifically tailored for the complexity of multi-modal biological and health data.

proposals.title: Generalizing Scalable Graph Convolutional Networks: A Benchmark for Web-Scale Social and E-Commerce Networks
proposals.motivation: Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) have shown promise for learning on web-scale graphs, as demonstrated in applications like Pinterest's PinSage. However, the generalizability and robustness of these scalable architectures across fundamentally different network domains—such as social graphs with dense, reciprocal interactions versus e-commerce graphs with sparse, heterogeneous user-item relationships—remain unvalidated. A systematic evaluation is crucial to understand the transferability of these methods and to guide future architecture design for massive real-world graphs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the scalable GCN architectures and sampling strategies developed for content recommendation (e.g., PinSage) can be effectively generalized to other web-scale domains, but will require domain-specific adaptations to node feature engineering and neighborhood sampling to achieve optimal performance.
proposals.method: We will adapt the core PinSage architecture—a scalable GCN using localized convolutions and efficient random-walk-based neighborhood sampling—to two new domains: a social network (Twitter follower graph) and an e-commerce network (Amazon product co-purchasing graph). We will implement task-specific decoders for link prediction and node classification, using GraphSAGE and Cluster-GCN as baseline scalable methods. Performance will be evaluated on large-scale, real-world subgraphs using metrics like recall@k and F1-score, with a focus on training efficiency and inference latency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, cross-domain benchmark for scalable GCNs, establishing performance baselines and identifying key architectural sensitivities to graph structure. It will deliver a practical framework and best practices for adapting state-of-the-art graph learning methods to new, massive network applications, bridging the gap between single-domain proofs-of-concept and general-purpose graph AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales GCNs to single large graphs, a systematic study of architectural transferability across diverse, web-scale network types—social and transactional—is novel. This moves beyond isolated case studies towards a principled understanding of generalizable graph learning.

proposals.title: Hypergraph Convolutional Networks for Multi-Relational Modeling of Single-Cell Perturbation Responses
proposals.motivation: Inference of cellular networks from single-cell perturbation data, as benchmarked in recent work, is fundamentally limited by pairwise interaction models. Real biological activity involves multi-way relationships, such as protein complexes or coordinated gene modules, which are poorly captured by standard graph methods. Hypergraphs provide a natural formalism to model these higher-order interactions, offering a more accurate representation of the underlying physical and regulatory processes within a cell.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling single-cell perturbation data as a hypergraph, where hyperedges represent multi-gene functional modules or co-regulated response programs, will yield more biologically plausible and predictive network models than pairwise graph approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a hypergraph convolutional network (HGCN) framework where nodes are genes/proteins and hyperedges are constructed from prior pathway databases (e.g., KEGG, Reactome) and inferred from co-expression patterns in perturbation data. We will adapt spectral hypergraph convolution operators (pre-2026, e.g., Feng et al. 2019) to propagate information across these multi-relation structures. The model will be trained on large-scale single-cell perturbation benchmarks to predict held-out gene expression outcomes, with the architecture designed to learn edge-dependent node representations reflecting different biological contexts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable method for multi-relation inference in biological systems, moving beyond pairwise edges. It will deliver a new benchmark for hypergraph-based learning in computational biology and offer interpretable, higher-order modules as evidence for complex cellular mechanisms derived from perturbation data.
proposals.novelty: While hypergraph methods exist, their application to the specific challenge of learning from single-cell perturbation data—integrating built-in biological knowledge with evidence from stochastic outcomes—is novel. This bridges Leskovec's themes of network methods across physical cell systems with recent focus on single-cell and protein interaction evidence.

proposals.title: Sparse, Modular Networks for Efficient and Generalizable Inference from Single-Cell Perturbation Data
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale AI models achieve broad capability through immense parameter counts, creating unsustainable computational and environmental costs. In parallel, the field of single-cell biology faces a similar challenge: inferring gene regulatory networks from perturbation data requires methods that are both highly expressive and sample-efficient, as biological systems themselves are sparse and modular. This project addresses the dual need for efficient, interpretable AI architectures and robust biological network inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neural architectures that algorithmically enforce sparsity and dynamic modularity, inspired by biological network organization, will achieve competitive or superior performance on complex reasoning and inference tasks (like gene network recovery) with significantly fewer parameters and less training data than dense, monolithic models.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel graph neural network (GNN) architecture where node-specific computation is performed by small, sparse, specialized sub-networks ("protein-inspired functional motifs") that are dynamically activated based on context. We will integrate sparsity via learned, input-dependent gating mechanisms (e.g., Top-k or L0 regularization variants) and enforce modularity through a differentiable clustering objective on latent node representations, encouraging a small-world structure. This model will be trained and benchmarked on large-scale single-cell perturbation datasets to infer causal gene interactions, directly leveraging Leskovec's expertise in network inference and graph representation learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, biologically-plausible neural architecture paradigm that prioritizes efficient connectivity over scale. It will provide a state-of-the-art, interpretable method for single-cell network inference, where learned modules may correspond to biological pathways. The results will offer concrete evidence for the viability of sparsity-driven scaling in AI.
proposals.novelty: While sparse networks exist, our approach uniquely co-designs architectural sparsity, dynamic module composition, and a network science-inspired modularity objective, explicitly translating principles from biological intelligence into a scalable AI framework. This moves beyond simple pruning or mixture-of-experts to learn an efficient, task-adaptive graph of computations.

proposals.title: Multilayer Network Inference from Single-Cell Perturbation Data: A Benchmark for Causal Structure Across Biological Scales
proposals.motivation: Biological systems are inherently multilayer, integrating physical protein-protein interactions, gene regulatory networks, and cellular communication. Pre-2026 network inference methods, including those benchmarked by Leskovec's group, typically operate on single-layer networks, failing to capture this integrative complexity. This limits our ability to model how perturbations propagate across different biological scales, a critical gap for systems biology and therapeutic discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multilayer network inference framework, leveraging ground-truth perturbation data across multiple relation types (e.g., protein-protein, gene regulatory), will significantly outperform single-layer methods in predicting causal cellular outcomes and will reveal novel cross-layer regulatory mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will extend the single-cell perturbation benchmark framework to construct a multilayer network with nodes representing biological entities (genes/proteins) and distinct layers for different relation types (physical, regulatory). We will employ tensor-based factorization methods and multilayer extensions of graph neural networks (e.g., multiplex GNNs) to infer intra- and inter-layer edges. The model will be trained and validated using high-quality, ground-truth datasets like Perturb-seq, combined with known physical interaction databases (e.g., STRING, BioGRID) to define layer semantics and provide partial supervision.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, standardized benchmark for multilayer network inference in biology, enabling rigorous evaluation of cross-scale predictive models. It will deliver a novel computational method that explicitly reasons across biological layers, offering a more holistic view of cellular activity and perturbation effects.
proposals.novelty: While multilayer network theory exists, its rigorous application to *inference* from single-cell perturbation data with established ground truth is novel. The integration of physical interaction evidence with functional regulatory data within a single learnable framework moves beyond post-hoc integration of separate network types.

proposals.title: Multi-Scale Graph Representation Learning for Interpretable Single-Cell Protein Function Prediction
proposals.motivation: In systems biology, predicting protein function from single-cell perturbation data is a core challenge, but existing graph neural networks often rely on flat pooling or pre-defined hierarchies that may not reflect the underlying biological activity. This limits their ability to capture the multi-scale physical and functional organization across cells, which is crucial for accurate and interpretable inference. A method that learns hierarchical, data-driven graph structure could better model the complex evidence built from perturbation responses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a graph neural network with a learned, hierarchical pooling mechanism will outperform standard methods on node-level prediction tasks in biological networks by explicitly capturing multi-scale functional contexts, and that the learned hierarchy will provide interpretable evidence for the predicted protein functions.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel hierarchical graph pooling architecture, extending differentiable pooling (DiffPool) or SAGPool concepts. The model will learn soft cluster assignments for nodes at multiple scales in an end-to-end fashion, conditioned on the graph structure and node features (e.g., protein expression from single-cell data). For the node-level task, we will use a combination of readout functions from each pooled scale, fused via attention, to inform final predictions for individual proteins. We will benchmark on pre-2026 single-cell perturbation datasets (e.g., from the 'network inference benchmark' paper) for tasks like essential protein identification or functional annotation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new graph learning method that directly leverages learned multi-scale structure for fine-grained node prediction, moving beyond simple neighborhood aggregation. It will provide a framework for interpreting biological predictions through the lens of the automatically discovered hierarchy, offering evidence for why certain proteins are predicted to have specific functions or roles in cellular activity.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical pooling exists for graph classification, its tailored application to inform *node-level* predictions in biological networks is novel. The key innovation is the explicit feedback of multi-scale, learned cluster memberships to refine individual node representations, rather than discarding node identity for a global graph summary.

proposals.title: Structure-Preserving Pooling via Multi-Scale Spectral Coarsening for Single-Cell and Protein Interaction Networks
proposals.motivation: Graph pooling methods for learning on biological networks, such as protein-protein interaction or single-cell signaling graphs, often rely on auxiliary link prediction or reconstruction losses to preserve crucial topological structure after coarsening. This multi-task setup introduces hyperparameter tuning burdens and can misalign the primary learning objective with structural fidelity. A pooling mechanism that inherently maintains connectivity patterns is essential for robust downstream tasks in computational biology, like inferring gene regulatory networks from perturbation data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a graph pooling objective based on directly optimizing for spectral similarity between the original and coarsened graph Laplacians will inherently preserve multiscale structural and functional properties, eliminating the need for an auxiliary link prediction loss.
proposals.method: We will develop a differentiable pooling operator that generates a coarsened adjacency matrix and node assignment by minimizing a multi-scale spectral divergence loss. This loss will measure the Wasserstein distance between the eigenvalues of the original graph's normalized Laplacian and the coarsened graph's lifted Laplacian, ensuring spectral similarity. The method will be integrated into a graph neural network (GNN) encoder and evaluated on pre-2026 benchmarks for single-cell network inference and protein function prediction, using only the primary task loss (e.g., cell state classification or protein activity prediction).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, task-agnostic graph pooling method that guarantees structural preservation for biological networks. It will simplify the training pipeline for GNNs in health and biology by removing a sensitive auxiliary objective, leading to more interpretable and stable representations for activity prediction across cells and proteins.
proposals.novelty: While spectral clustering informs pooling, directly using spectral divergence as the primary, differentiable objective for learning node assignments in an end-to-end GNN is novel. This moves beyond using spectral features as mere inputs or employing reconstruction-based auxiliary losses.

proposals.title: HeteroPool: Hierarchical Graph Pooling for Multi-Relational Biological Networks
proposals.motivation: Biological systems, such as protein-protein interaction networks and single-cell perturbation graphs, are inherently multi-relational, with diverse node (e.g., genes, proteins, cells) and edge types (e.g., physical interaction, regulatory, co-expression). Pre-2026 graph neural networks (GNNs) and pooling methods, including DiffPool, often struggle with this heterogeneity, forcing a homogenization that discards critical biological semantics. This limits their ability to infer meaningful hierarchical structures and causal relationships from complex biomedical data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical pooling mechanism that explicitly models the distinct roles of heterogeneous node and edge types will learn more biologically meaningful and robust graph representations, leading to superior performance in downstream tasks like network inference and protein function prediction.
proposals.method: We will extend the DiffPool framework to operate on heterogeneous graphs. Our method, HeteroPool, will employ type-specific encoder networks to project nodes of different types into a unified latent space, while using relation-aware attention mechanisms to compute soft cluster assignments. The pooling operation will be regularized by a novel edge-type preservation loss, encouraging the coarsened graph to maintain the multi-relational structure of the original network. We will validate the approach on pre-2026 benchmarks for single-cell perturbation response and multi-scale biological networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, general-purpose pooling operator for heterogeneous graphs, filling a key gap in geometric deep learning. It will deliver a new benchmark for hierarchical representation learning on complex biological systems, with direct applications in distilling evidence from multi-modal health data and built knowledge graphs for improved biological reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on heterogeneous GNNs (e.g., RGCN, HAN) focuses on representation learning, and standard pooling methods handle homogeneous graphs, HeteroPool is novel in integrating explicit type semantics directly into the hierarchical graph coarsening process, a critical step for multi-scale analysis.

proposals.title: Capsule-Graph Networks: Hierarchical Part Discovery for Interpretable Biological Network Inference
proposals.motivation: Inferring interpretable, hierarchical structure from biological networks, such as protein-protein interaction or single-cell perturbation graphs, is a core challenge in computational biology. Current graph neural networks (GNNs) often produce flat, entangled representations that obscure the multi-scale functional modules (e.g., protein complexes, signaling pathways) crucial for mechanistic understanding. This limits their utility in generating testable biological hypotheses from high-throughput data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting capsule network routing mechanisms to graph-structured data will enable the automatic, data-driven discovery of hierarchical, semantically consistent parts (graph capsules), leading to more interpretable and robust models for network inference tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop Capsule-Graph Networks (CapsGNs), extending the dynamic routing-by-agreement algorithm from CapsNets to operate on graph nodes. Lower-level node features will be routed into a smaller set of higher-level graph capsules, each representing a latent subgraph part. We will specifically test this architecture on the task of inferring gene regulatory networks from single-cell perturbation benchmark data, evaluating if the learned capsules correspond to known biological modules. The model will be trained end-to-end using a reconstruction loss and a margin loss for capsule existence.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel graph neural architecture capable of explicit hierarchical part-whole decomposition. It will provide a new method for interpretable biological network inference, moving beyond black-box predictions to generate structured, multi-scale hypotheses about cellular organization. The resulting model and benchmarks will advance methods for discovering actionable evidence from complex biological systems.
proposals.novelty: While capsule networks have been applied to images and simple graphs, their principled adaptation for hierarchical pooling and part discovery in complex, attributed biological networks is novel. This approach directly addresses the need for interpretable structure learning, a gap in pre-2026 GNN methods focused primarily on predictive accuracy.

proposals.title: MetaPool: Meta-Learning Adaptive Graph Pooling Architectures for Generalizable Biological Network Analysis
proposals.motivation: Graph pooling is critical for learning hierarchical representations in biological networks, such as protein-protein interaction or single-cell signaling graphs. However, existing pooling strategies (e.g., DiffPool, SAGPool) are manually designed and often fail to generalize across diverse tasks, from predicting protein function to inferring perturbation responses from single-cell data. A method to automatically discover task-adaptive pooling architectures would significantly improve robustness and performance in computational biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework can discover latent principles of effective graph coarsening, enabling the learning of pooling architectures that generalize better across diverse graph prediction tasks than any fixed, hand-designed strategy.
proposals.method: We will develop a gradient-based meta-learning (MAML) framework where the inner-loop learns task-specific graph neural networks, and the outer-loop optimizes a controller network that generates the pooling architecture (e.g., layer-wise node selection ratios and aggregation functions). The model will be meta-trained on a diverse suite of pre-2026 graph benchmarks (e.g., TUDatasets, Open Graph Benchmark) and biologically relevant networks (protein, cell-cell interaction). The controller's ability to generalize will be tested on held-out tasks from the domains of health and built environment networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel meta-learning framework for adaptive graph pooling, 2) empirical evidence on the generalizability of learned pooling strategies across physical, biological, and social networks, and 3) open-source code and pre-trained controllers for the community to apply to new graph tasks, advancing methods for single-cell and protein network analysis.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been applied to optimize GNN hyperparameters, its use to discover fundamental, transferable graph *pooling architectures*—moving beyond fixed schemes or simple search spaces—is novel and directly addresses the generalization challenge highlighted in recent network inference benchmarks.

proposals.title: Structure-Aware Micro-Pooling for Representation Learning on Small Biological Networks
proposals.motivation: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are powerful for analyzing biological systems, from protein-protein interaction networks to single-cell signaling graphs. However, standard pooling methods (e.g., DiffPool, SAGPool) are designed for larger graphs and often fail or lose critical information when applied to very small graphs (e.g., 3-20 nodes), which are common in modeling localized cellular processes or small molecular complexes. This gap limits our ability to build predictive models for fine-grained biological activity from single-cell perturbation data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pooling method explicitly designed for very small graphs, which preserves local topological motifs and node feature distributions, will yield significantly more informative graph-level representations for downstream tasks like protein function prediction or perturbation outcome classification.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel micro-pooling layer that operates on learned soft assignments of nodes to a small, adaptive number of meta-nodes. The method will incorporate: 1) A structure-aware attention mechanism that weights nodes based on their centrality and role in small, pre-defined motifs (e.g., triangles, stars) identified within the tiny graph. 2) A regularization term that discourages excessive compression, ensuring no single node's features are lost. 3) Integration with a GNN backbone (e.g., GIN) to enable end-to-end learning. We will benchmark against existing pooling methods on small-graph datasets derived from protein structures and single-cell perturbation networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated, theoretically-grounded pooling operator for very small graphs, enabling more accurate modeling of compact biological systems. It will contribute a new benchmark for small-graph representation learning and release an open-source implementation integrated with popular GNN libraries.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing pooling strategies that assume reducibility, our method explicitly models the irreducibility of tiny graphs, treating pooling as a structure-preserving refinement rather than a coarse-graining operation. This addresses a fundamental oversight in geometric deep learning for biology.

proposals.title: Generative Hierarchical Graph Models: Inverting Assignment Matrices for Controlled Structure Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Current graph generation models often lack explicit, interpretable control over the multi-scale community structure of generated networks, which is critical for modeling real-world systems like protein interaction networks or built environment activity flows. Pre-2026 hierarchical graph representation learning methods, such as those using differentiable assignment matrices, excel at *inferring* structure but are not designed for the *inverse problem* of generating new, plausible graphs from a specified hierarchy. This limits their utility in hypothesis testing and data augmentation for domains like single-cell biology, where generating realistic perturbation networks is essential.
proposals.hypothesis: The hierarchical assignment matrices learned by models like differentiable graph pooling can be inverted into a generative process, enabling the synthesis of novel graph structures that adhere to a specified multi-scale organization while preserving key local statistical properties.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep generative framework where a target hierarchy, defined as a sequence of soft cluster assignment matrices, conditions a graph decoder. The core architecture will be a hierarchical variational autoencoder (VAE) or a diffusion model. The encoder will be a pre-trained, differentiable pooling operator (e.g., from DiffPool or MinCutPool) that produces assignment matrices. The decoder will be designed to invert this process, using the assignment matrices to guide the generation of adjacency matrices through a series of stochastic upsampling and edge-prediction layers, regularized by graphons or implicit neural representations to ensure global consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new paradigm for controlled graph generation with explicit hierarchical constraints, directly applicable to generating synthetic benchmarks for network inference tasks in systems biology. It will also offer a novel lens for interpreting what hierarchical graph neural networks have learned, by examining the space of graphs they can generate from a given latent hierarchy.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical graph representation learning is established, using the learned hierarchy as a *generative blueprint* is a novel inversion of its typical discriminative use. This approach moves beyond prior generative models that learn hierarchies implicitly, by making the multi-scale structure an explicit, controllable input to the generation process.

proposals.title: Seq2Graph: A Generative Sequence-to-Sequence Framework for Conditional Protein-Protein Interaction Network Assembly
proposals.motivation: Inferring physical protein-protein interaction (PPI) networks from single-cell perturbation data is a core challenge in systems biology, but current graph neural network (GNN) methods are often limited to classification or link prediction. A generative model that can output plausible, context-specific network substructures as sequences would enable the systematic hypothesis generation of cellular mechanisms, directly addressing the need for causal reasoning from perturbation evidence highlighted in recent benchmarks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequence-to-sequence model, trained to translate a sequence of perturbed protein identifiers and cell-state features into a sequence representing a local PPI subgraph, can generate biologically plausible and context-relevant interaction networks more effectively than static, discriminative GNN approaches.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Transformer encoder-decoder architecture. The encoder will process a sequence of input tokens representing a perturbation condition (e.g., knocked-down genes) and aggregated single-cell readouts. The decoder will autoregressively generate a sequence that, when parsed using a defined grammar, constructs an adjacency list or a linearized graph traversal of a predicted PPI subgraph. We will train the model on large-scale, single-cell perturbation PPI datasets, using teacher forcing and a loss function combining token prediction accuracy and a graph-based reward (e.g., via policy gradient from a GNN-based critic network) to ensure structural validity.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, generative deep learning framework for conditional biological network assembly, moving beyond inference to constructive prediction. It will provide a new tool for in-silico experimentation in systems biology, allowing researchers to propose testable network hypotheses from perturbation data. The method also advances the technical frontier by effectively bridging sequence-based reasoning with structured graph generation.
proposals.novelty: While sequence models generate text and graphs are modeled with GNNs, directly applying a seq2seq paradigm to generate interpretable graph sequences conditioned on cellular activity data is a novel synthesis. This approach departs from the standard use of GNNs for single-graph outputs or VAEs for whole-graph generation, focusing instead on conditional, sequence-based construction of meaningful network substructures.

proposals.title: Causal Graph Embeddings for Predicting Cellular Intervention Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Graph embeddings are powerful for predicting node and link properties, but standard methods conflate correlation with causation, limiting their utility for predicting the effects of interventions. This is critical in domains like single-cell biology, where inferring the causal impact of a gene knockout or drug perturbation from observational and limited interventional data is essential for understanding cellular activity and guiding therapeutic discovery. Current methods, including those benchmarked in Leskovec's recent work, often lack explicit causal structure, leading to unreliable predictions for 'what-if' scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating causal inference principles—specifically, by learning graph embeddings that respect a structural causal model (SCM)—will yield representations that significantly outperform standard embeddings in predicting the outcomes of targeted interventions on biological networks.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel framework, Causal Graph Neural Network (CausalGNN), that integrates a differentiable causal layer. The model will be trained on a combination of large-scale observational single-cell RNA-seq data and targeted perturbation data (e.g., from CRISPR screens). We will use a variational autoencoder (VAE) architecture to learn latent embeddings, where the encoder is a GNN and the decoder is conditioned on an inferred SCM representing gene regulatory interactions. The loss function will combine reconstruction loss with a causal regularization term derived from the do-calculus, encouraging the embeddings to be invariant to distribution shifts induced by simulated interventions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new class of graph embedding methods capable of robustly answering causal queries, directly applicable to predicting protein function and cellular activity post-intervention. It will deliver a benchmark and open-source framework for causal representation learning on graphs, advancing the field from retrieval and correlation-based reasoning to causal reasoning for knowledge discovery in biology.
proposals.novelty: While causal inference and graph embeddings are mature fields, their integration to create intervention-aware embeddings is nascent. Our approach is novel in explicitly enforcing causal invariances within the embedding space of a GNN, moving beyond using graphs merely as a correlation structure to treat them as a scaffold for causal models.

proposals.title: Graph Attention Networks for Interpretable Inference in Single-Cell Perturbation Biology
proposals.motivation: Inferring gene regulatory networks from single-cell perturbation data is a core challenge in systems biology, but existing methods often treat all neighbor interactions in a protein-protein interaction graph equally. This limits their ability to identify context-specific, causal pathways underlying cellular activity. An attention-based weighting mechanism, proven transformative in NLP for sequence modeling, could provide a more nuanced and interpretable model of biological influence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the self-attention mechanism to graph-structured data will create a Graph Attention Network (GAT) that can dynamically weight the importance of neighboring genes or proteins, leading to more accurate and biologically interpretable predictions of perturbation outcomes.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Transformer's scaled dot-product attention to operate on graph neighborhoods. For each node (e.g., a gene), the model will compute attention coefficients over its first-order neighbors in a prior knowledge graph (e.g., STRING), using a shared, learnable linear transformation. A multi-head attention mechanism will stabilize learning. We will train this GAT architecture on large-scale single-cell RNA-seq perturbation benchmark datasets (e.g., from our prior work) to predict gene expression changes, using graph convolution layers to propagate these weighted signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, interpretable deep learning architecture for biological network inference. It will advance methods for analyzing single-cell perturbation data by providing explicit attention weights that quantify the influence strength between interacting entities, offering built-in evidence for hypothesized regulatory edges. The model will be benchmarked against existing inference methods to demonstrate superior performance.
proposals.novelty: While Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) use fixed, pre-defined neighborhood weighting, our approach introduces a trainable, node-specific attention mechanism to graphs, making the relative importance of connections a central, learnable component of the model. This moves beyond simple aggregation to dynamic, context-aware reasoning on biological networks.

proposals.title: EdgeCell: Ultra-Compact Graph Embeddings for Single-Cell Phenotype Inference on Wearable Devices
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of single-cell and protein interaction data in health diagnostics creates a need for real-time, on-device inference, such as predicting cell state from perturbation responses. However, deploying graph neural networks (GNNs) for this on resource-constrained edge and wearable devices is infeasible due to the memory and compute overhead of standard node and graph embeddings. Current compression methods sacrifice too much predictive accuracy for biological tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing a knowledge distillation framework with learned, adaptive graph sparsification, we can produce ultra-compact graph representations that retain the predictive power of full-scale models for inference tasks like protein function or cell activity prediction.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method. First, we will adapt techniques from the GRPO and reasoning agent literature to train a lightweight student GNN via policy-guided distillation from a large teacher model, using evidence from biological networks as a reward signal. Second, we will integrate a novel, trainable graph coarsening layer that learns to merge nodes (e.g., proteins or cells) into super-nodes based on activity and physical interaction patterns, producing a miniaturized but semantically rich graph for the final embedding step. Evaluation will use benchmarks for network inference from single-cell perturbation data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for generating sub-100KB graph embeddings suitable for microcontroller-class hardware. It will provide new evidence on the trade-offs between compression, sparsification, and accuracy in biomedical graph models. The resulting open-source toolkit will enable new built-environment and mobile health applications.
proposals.novelty: While distillation and sparsification exist, their combination with adaptive, task-aware graph coarsening for biological networks is unexplored. The approach uniquely targets the constraints of edge devices while leveraging recent advances in single-cell benchmarks and reasoning-aware training.

proposals.title: DualGrad: Inferring Cell State Embeddings from Model Gradients in Single-Cell Perturbation Networks
proposals.motivation: Inferring causal networks from single-cell perturbation data is a core challenge in systems biology, but current methods often rely on static statistical correlations. The gradients of a model trained to predict a cell's response to perturbation encode rich, task-specific information about the functional relationships between genes and proteins. Leveraging this dynamic signal could provide more robust embeddings of cellular states, directly linking to the physical activity of biological networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the gradient field of a predictive model (e.g., a Graph Neural Network) trained on single-cell perturbation data contains a compressed, informative signal that can be distilled into stable node (gene/protein) and graph (cell state) embeddings, which will outperform embeddings derived from static features alone for downstream tasks like drug effect prediction.
proposals.method: We will train a GNN on large-scale single-cell perturbation benchmark data to predict post-perturbation expression profiles. For each node (gene) in the inferred biological network, we will compute its gradient with respect to the model's loss on specific perturbation tasks. We will then develop a dual encoder architecture: one network processes the original node features, while a second, concurrent network processes the per-node gradient tensors. These representations will be fused via attention mechanisms to produce final embeddings, evaluated on held-out perturbation prediction and novel drug effect classification.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel paradigm for deriving biological embeddings from model dynamics rather than static data. It will provide a new method for network inference that integrates evidence from model sensitivity, potentially revealing more causal relationships. The resulting 'gradient embeddings' could serve as a powerful foundation model component for health-related prediction tasks in computational biology.
proposals.novelty: While gradient-based meta-learning (e.g., MAML) and influence functions exist, using raw model gradients as direct input features for constructing biological network embeddings is unexplored. This approach dualizes the model training process itself into a source of representation, moving beyond the analysis of just model parameters or outputs.

proposals.title: Temporal Graph Embeddings with Adaptive Forgetting for Evolving Biological Networks
proposals.motivation: Dynamic networks in biology, such as evolving protein-protein interaction maps or cell-cell communication graphs from longitudinal single-cell data, exhibit concept drift where the underlying relational patterns change over time. Pre-2026 graph embedding methods often struggle with never-ending streams, either by catastrophically forgetting old patterns or failing to adapt to new ones, limiting their utility for real-time health monitoring and inference. A method that can continuously update embeddings while strategically forgetting outdated information is crucial for tracking dynamic biological processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a streaming graph embedding framework with a theoretically grounded, adaptive forgetting mechanism will outperform static and naive incremental methods in maintaining predictive accuracy for downstream tasks like link prediction and node classification in the presence of concept drift.
proposals.method: We will extend the GraphSAGE/GraphSAINT architecture to a streaming setting, where new edges and nodes arrive in batches. The core novelty is a learnable, node-specific 'memory decay' parameter integrated into the temporal loss function, inspired by elastic weight consolidation but adapted for graph-structured data. This parameter will be regulated by a drift detector monitoring the distribution of embedding updates, forcing selective forgetting of stale connections while consolidating stable, evidence-backed topological features. We will benchmark on dynamic protein interaction networks and simulated single-cell perturbation cascades.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, scalable algorithm for lifelong learning on graph streams with demonstrable applications in computational biology. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating temporal embedding stability and accuracy under drift. The method will enable more accurate, real-time inference of latent biological states from streaming experimental data.
proposals.novelty: While incremental graph embedding exists, explicitly modeling and learning *what to forget* in a streaming biological context is novel. The integration of a parameterized forgetting mechanism directly into the graph neural network's objective, guided by concept drift detection, moves beyond simple sliding windows or rehearsal buffers.

ideas.title: Algorithmic Sparsity and Modularity: Achieving Capability via Efficient Architecture, Not Scale
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that scale (in parameters, data, and compute) is the only path to broad AI capability. By drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology and neuroscience, we note that biological intelligence scaled not by uniformly adding more generic neurons, but by developing sparse, modular, and highly efficient architectures (e.g., small-world networks, sparse coding, specialized functional circuits). The analogous solution is this move from brute-force scaling to optimized, efficient connectivity and specialization. We adapt this solution to AI by proposing the investigation of novel algorithmic architectures that intrinsically enforce high sparsity, dynamic modularity, and task-specialized circuits. The adapted idea is to design systems where broad capability emerges from the quality, efficiency, and intelligent organization of connections and modules, not merely their quantity. This could involve research into next-generation sparse neural models, systems that learn to compose and re-use modular functions efficiently, or architectures inspired by the brain's cost-effective scaling strategies, aiming to achieve significant capabilities without reliance on massive scale.

ideas.title: Composable Expert Ensembles with Domain-Optimized Data Pipelines
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the co-design of data curation strategies and model architecture for collaborative AI systems. Instead of a single model trained on broad data, the approach develops multiple, highly efficient expert models, each trained on a meticulously curated, narrow-domain dataset optimized for factual reliability and task-specific performance. A central challenge is designing a meta-framework—encompassing routing, composition, and consensus mechanisms—that allows these heterogeneous experts to dynamically form ad-hoc ensembles to tackle novel, multi-faceted problems. The synergy lies in using narrow-domain data to create supremely competent specialists, while the collaborative architecture bypasses the limitations of any single model's domain, creating a flexible and trustworthy system that is more than the sum of its specialized parts.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Robustness in Hyper-Connected AI Systems via Strategic Sensor Placement
ideas.description: This research develops novel evaluation benchmarks for AI robustness by fusing concepts from sensor placement in dense networks with systematic failure assessment. It models complex AI systems (e.g., multi-agent systems, modular neural networks, or AI-human workflows) as near-complete graphs where information or failure spreads rapidly via shortcut edges. The core method involves strategically placing 'sensors' (evaluation probes) and 'stressors' (controlled perturbations) at theoretically optimal nodes to efficiently trigger and observe cascading failures across the hyper-connected system. This approach moves beyond static test sets to create dynamic, topology-aware evaluation protocols that systematically uncover latent robustness flaws and emergent failure modes in interconnected AI.

ideas.title: Constellation of Curated Experts with Meta-Reasoning for Robust Narrow-Domain Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid architecture to overcome the brittleness of single-domain foundation models. It develops a system of multiple, highly efficient models, each trained on deeply curated data for a specific narrow domain (e.g., organic chemistry, legal contracts, medical imaging). A central meta-reasoning model, trained on broad cross-domain tasks and failure patterns, orchestrates this constellation by routing inputs, combining expert outputs, and identifying out-of-distribution scenarios. This approach aims to deliver the reliability and efficiency of narrow curation while maintaining the adaptive robustness needed for practical, real-world application.

ideas.title: Active Learning for Dynamic Network Inference with Non-Monotonic Spread
ideas.description: This research direction develops sequential sensor placement algorithms that fuse active learning query strategies with non-progressive spread models (e.g., SIS, SIR, or threshold models with recovery). The core innovation is an adaptive observation policy where each selected sensor location is chosen not only to reduce uncertainty about the current state of the spread but, more critically, to best inform the unknown parameters of the dynamic network process itself—such as transmission rates, recovery rates, or latent network structure. This enables highly efficient inference and forecasting for phenomena where infection can recede, unlike simple progressive models, by treating the monitoring campaign as an optimal experiment design problem for learning the model online. The result is a closed-loop system that strategically probes a dynamic process to rapidly learn how it evolves and where it will likely manifest next.

ideas.title: Evaluating Non-Transfer-Learning Adaptation for Micro-Scale Pathogen Emergence Detection
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that fine-tuning is the optimal adaptation method by applying and comparing alternative paradigms to the specific, high-stakes problem of detecting micro-scale pathogen emergence within cellular or tissue networks. It leverages signal propagation detection methodologies from network science to model early infection dynamics at a biological scale, where data is extremely sparse and the cost of missed detection is catastrophic. The core innovation is using this concrete, demanding application as a benchmark to evaluate whether methods like prompt-based tuning, model reprogramming, or sparse network adaptation can outperform standard transfer learning in speed, data efficiency, and generalization to novel threats. The outcome is both a potential breakthrough in early outbreak detection and a principled evaluation framework for adaptation strategies in critical, low-data domains.

ideas.title: Algorithmic Sensor Minimization for Guaranteed Detection via Architectural Efficiency
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that reliable, time-bound detection of complex events (e.g., network intrusions, environmental anomalies) requires massive, dense sensor arrays and the large-scale models trained on their data. Instead, it seeks to design novel, lightweight neural or algorithmic architectures that actively learn optimal, minimal sensor placements and fuse their sparse, sequential data under strict latency constraints. The core synergy is that the guarantee of detection within a bounded time forces the development of non-standard, efficient architectures that achieve high capability through algorithmic ingenuity rather than scale, directly testing the limits of what small, smart systems can guarantee.

ideas.title: Efficient Multi-Model Collaboration via a Co-Trained Dynamic Orchestrator
ideas.description: This system integrates multiple specialized neural models with a lightweight, neural orchestrator that is trained concurrently with the specialists. The orchestrator learns to dynamically select, combine, and route information among specialists based on the input, minimizing unnecessary computation and inter-model communication. This end-to-end training, which includes a penalty for latency and bandwidth, ensures the collaborative system is Pareto-superior to a monolithic model, offering better performance without sacrificing efficiency or reliability. The architecture enables seamless scaling and updating of individual specialists without retraining the entire ensemble.

ideas.title: Collaborative Model Ecosystems via Metabolic Pathway Principles
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that a single, homogenized foundation model is necessary for complex AI capabilities. Drawing an analogy from systems biology, it adapts the solution of metabolic pathways—where networks of specialized enzymes collaborate to perform complex biochemical functions—to the AI context. The analogous solution shows how modular, specialized components can be coordinated to achieve robust, emergent functionality without a monolithic design. The adapted idea proposes designing collaborative AI ecosystems where task execution is decomposed into pathways of specialized model 'modules.' A central routing and coordination mechanism, inspired by cellular regulatory logic, dynamically assembles these modules into workflows, passing intermediate data representations between them. This approach relaxes the necessity for parameter homogenization, allowing for extreme model specialization, efficient independent updates, and the composition of complex capabilities from simpler, interoperable parts, potentially leading to more efficient, adaptable, and interpretable AI systems.

ideas.title: Adversarially Robust Outbreak Detection via Task-Specific Model Synthesis
ideas.description: This research investigates the co-design of outbreak detection sensor networks and the machine learning models that process their data, under the threat of an intelligent adversary who can probe and adapt to the system's adaptation paradigm. It challenges the assumption that transfer learning (e.g., fine-tuning a pre-trained model) is always the optimal adaptation strategy for deploying detection algorithms in new, high-stakes environments like cities or networks. Instead, the work will rigorously compare alternative paradigms—such as training from scratch, using ensemble methods, or employing novel model synthesis techniques—specifically evaluating their robustness when an adversary designs contaminant spread paths to evade not just sensor locations, but the known weaknesses of the deployed model's learning architecture. The core novelty is formulating and solving a bilevel optimization problem where the outer loop selects the adaptation paradigm and sensor placement, and the inner loop simulates an adversary optimally exploiting the chosen paradigm's vulnerabilities.

ideas.title: Sensor Placement via Metabolic Network Critical Node Analysis
ideas.description: This research develops methods for optimal sensor placement in extremely dense networks (near-complete graphs) where shortcut edges make the spread of information, faults, or contagions nearly instantaneous. Drawing an analogy from Systems Biology, specifically metabolic network analysis, we adapt the solution of identifying 'minimal cut sets' and 'choke points'—critical enzymes/metabolites whose removal maximally disrupts flux in a highly interconnected reaction network. The adapted method will focus on analyzing the potential flow pathways and redundancy in the dense communication graph to computationally identify a minimal set of critical nodes. Placing sensors at these choke points aims to optimally monitor or control the system-wide spread despite the challenge posed by instantaneous connections, transforming the problem from one of coverage to one of strategic bottleneck identification.

ideas.title: Collaborative Multi-Model Systems with Probabilistic Sensor-Aware Fusion
ideas.description: This research direction designs collaborative AI systems where multiple specialized perception or reasoning models, each potentially trained on different data or architectures, operate in concert without requiring parameter homogenization. Crucially, each model receives input from sensor modalities explicitly modeled as unreliable, with known probabilities for missed detections and false alarms. The system's core innovation is a meta-reasoning layer that dynamically fuses the heterogeneous models' outputs by weighting their contributions based on both their inherent competencies and the current estimated reliability of their respective sensor feeds. This enables graceful degradation and robust performance in real-world conditions where sensors may fail intermittently, moving beyond static fusion to context-aware, uncertainty-resilient collaborative intelligence.

ideas.title: A Formal Taxonomy and Benchmark for Catastrophic Forgetting and Distributional Robustness in High-Stakes Model Adaptation
ideas.description: This research posits that fine-tuning fails catastrophically for high-stakes adaptation when task data is extremely sparse and the distribution shift induces novel, critical failure modes not present in the source data. To test this, we develop a benchmark that formally defines and measures two key risks: catastrophic forgetting of crucial source-domain safeguards and brittle in-distribution performance under adversarial distribution shifts. The system then evaluates a suite of adaptation methods—including fine-tuning, linear probes, adapter layers, and prompt-based learning—against this benchmark across simulated high-stakes scenarios (e.g., medical imaging with rare conditions, financial anomaly detection) to produce a prescriptive decision framework.

ideas.title: Minimal Dominating Set for Bounded Detection Time in Sensor Networks
ideas.description: This research adapts the Minimum Dominating Set (MDS) problem from graph theory to solve the sensor placement and activation problem with a strict temporal guarantee. The analogous solution involves finding the smallest set of vertices that dominate all others in a graph. We adapt this by constructing a spatio-temporal network model where vertices represent potential sensor locations or regions, and edges represent detection coverage and communication links annotated with time delays (sensing latency, transmission time). The objective is to compute the minimal set of sensors to activate such that, for any point in the space, there exists a path from a sensor covering that point to the base station where the cumulative delay of sensing and communication is ≤ T. This transforms the original idea into a 'Minimum Temporal Dominating Set' problem, balancing spatial coverage with temporal constraints in the network's delay structure.

ideas.title: Resource-Constrained Adaptive Sensor Scheduling for Guaranteed Timely Detection
ideas.description: This research develops algorithms to find the smallest possible set of mobile sensors that can be dynamically repositioned over time to guarantee the detection of any threat within a pre-specified, strict time bound. It unifies the static optimization for minimal sensor count with the dynamic need for adaptation as network connectivity or threat propagation models evolve. The core challenge is to co-optimize the initial placement and a schedule of future redeployments, ensuring the time-guarantee is never violated despite changes in the environment. This leads to novel problems in temporal graph covering and robust, receding-horizon scheduling under resource constraints.

ideas.title: Scale up data quality, not just quantity

ideas.title: Domain-Specific Immunization: Using Curated Data to Build Robust and Efficient Foundational Models
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that foundation models require broad, general-purpose training data by drawing an analogy from vaccinology. The analogous problem is how to safely and effectively train an immune system. The field moved from broad, attenuated exposures (live vaccines) to curated, narrow components (subunit vaccines) for a more targeted and reliable response. Adapting this solution, the research will investigate if a foundation model trained on a highly curated, narrow-domain 'subunit' of data (e.g., rigorously verified scientific literature) can achieve more reliable, efficient, and robust performance within that domain than a broadly-trained model, while minimizing the production of incorrect or nonsensical outputs ('hallucinations').

ideas.title: Dynamic Adversarial Evaluation Ecosystem for Causal Failure Diagnosis and Robustness Generalization
ideas.description: This research develops an evaluation paradigm centered on a dynamic, closed-loop ecosystem where adaptive adversarial agents continuously generate novel and evolving challenges to probe model weaknesses. It introduces metrics for robustness generalization, quantifying how well a model's performance on known failure modes predicts its performance on unforeseen, evolved attacks and distribution shifts. The core output is not just a score but a causal diagnostic framework that traces failures back to specific model capabilities or architectural choices, enabling targeted improvements. This moves evaluation from a static snapshot to a continuous stress test that mirrors real-world adversarial dynamics.

ideas.title: Dynamic Inference-Time Guardrails for Behavioral Constraint Without Catastrophic Unlearning
ideas.description: This research develops a method to mitigate harmful model behaviors without modifying the core model's weights, thus avoiding catastrophic unlearning and entanglement issues. A small, trainable guardrail network is attached to the frozen base model and trained through adversarial reinforcement to intercept and correct the model's internal activations during generation. The system is optimized to satisfy explicit safety and fairness constraints on the output distribution, making the model robustly aligned without erasing its underlying knowledge. This results in a composable safety layer that can be updated or tailored without expensive retraining of the main model.

ideas.title: Adaptive Compressive Sensing with Learned Sparsity for Robust Sparse Monitoring
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond the rigid sparsity assumption of traditional compressive sensing by embedding a representation learning stage. The system uses a limited corpus of historical sensor data to jointly train an autoencoder for discovering an optimally sparse latent representation and a reconstruction network that maps compressive measurements to this latent space. An online module continuously validates reconstruction fidelity against a small set of trusted reference measurements, triggering adaptation of the latent basis if performance degrades. This creates a robust monitoring framework that maintains high detection accuracy even for complex, evolving signals where the sparsity structure is initially unknown.

ideas.title: Modular Adaptation for High-Stakes AI: A Biological Immune System Analogy
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that transfer learning via fine-tuning is always optimal for adaptation in high-stakes AI applications. Drawing an analogy from immunology and vaccine development, where the body uses a modular response (deploying specific antibodies) rather than overhauling the entire immune system, we propose an alternative adaptation paradigm. The analogous solution involves maintaining a stable core system while rapidly composing specialized, verifiable modules to handle novel threats. Adapted to machine learning, this translates to developing a framework where foundational models remain frozen, and adaptation occurs through the dynamic composition, selection, or switching of small, task-specific 'adapter' modules. This modular approach is investigated for applications like medical diagnosis or autonomous systems, where it may outperform fine-tuning by offering greater robustness, easier auditability, reduced catastrophic forgetting, and safer failure modes, even if it requires more sophisticated architectural design.

ideas.title: Strategic Monitoring for Adversarial Spread in Dense Foundation Model Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research investigates the security of large-scale, interconnected foundation model deployment environments, such as those with shared fine-tuning services, model hubs, or federated learning setups, which form extremely dense logical networks. It adapts sensor placement and influence maximization principles from physical network theory to this digital domain, developing methods to optimally position lightweight monitoring probes or hardened sub-models. These strategically placed sensors are designed to detect the rapid, nearly instantaneous spread of adversarial perturbations, backdoors, or poisoned data artifacts across the ecosystem. The resulting system enables early warning, attack attribution, and containment strategies tailored for the unique propagation dynamics of AI model vulnerabilities, moving beyond static defenses to dynamic, topology-aware security.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Distributionally Robust Optimization for Parameter Uncertainty
ideas.description: This approach addresses the conservatism of classical robust optimization by leveraging data to inform the uncertainty model. It constructs an ambiguity set of probability distributions (e.g., via φ-divergence or Wasserstein distance) that are consistent with observed data. The system then solves a minimax problem over this set of distributions, yielding decisions that are robust to distributional mis-specification while maintaining strong average-case performance. This provides a principled, less conservative hedge against uncertainty where the true parameter distribution is unknown but not purely adversarial.

ideas.title: The Capability-Compute Frontier: A Rigorous Benchmark for Measuring Architectural Efficiency Gains
ideas.description: This research program rigorously tests whether algorithmic innovations can significantly shift the Pareto frontier of capabilities versus compute. It establishes a fixed, low compute budget and a diverse benchmark suite spanning mathematical reasoning, code generation, and grounded QA. The core methodology involves co-designing novel architectures (e.g., hybrid symbolic-neural, structured state spaces) and training algorithms via automated search, then performing controlled ablation studies against scaled Transformer baselines. Success is quantitatively defined as achieving a higher multi-task performance percentile within the strict compute constraint than any scaled-up model, providing a falsifiable metric for challenging 'scale-only' scaling laws.

ideas.title: Integrity-Aware Online Learning with Sensor Stream Validation for Robust Detection
ideas.description: This system integrates optimal sensor placement with a robust online learning framework that actively defends against data stream corruption. It employs a parallel validation module that continuously tests the information content and consistency of real-time sensor readings using techniques like predictive coding and anomaly detection on data embeddings. If a stream is flagged as degraded or adversarial, the system can dynamically reweight its contribution, trigger calibration protocols, or fall back to a trusted prior model, ensuring the core detection task remains reliable. This creates a closed-loop system where sensor data not only informs detection but is also itself scrutinized, making the overall integration resilient to real-world sensor failures and environmental shifts.

ideas.title: Causal Layer Inference for Events in Fast-Mixing Network Sinks
ideas.description: This research develops methods to infer event origins and dynamics when the final propagation layer is a dense, fast-mixing network that obscures traditional sensor signals. Instead of placing sensors within the dense graph, the approach identifies the sparse feeder networks or causal chains that inject events into it. The core technical contribution is a framework to optimally place sensors in these upstream layers to enable Bayesian inference of source characteristics, leveraging the dense network's mixing properties as a known, instantaneous transformation rather than a medium to be monitored.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning for Dynamic Sensor Failure Regime Identification and Adaptation
ideas.description: This system models sensor unreliability not with fixed probabilities but by dynamically identifying the active failure regime from a set of possible pathologies, including novel ones. It employs a hierarchical Bayesian framework that compares predictions across sensor modalities and over time to detect inconsistencies and classify their pattern. The core model then adapts its fusion and state estimation strategy in real-time, selecting the most credible sensor data and appropriately inflating uncertainty under ambiguous failure signatures. This results in a perception system that is robust to unforeseen sensor degradations and adversarial interference, maintaining graceful degradation rather than catastrophic failure.

ideas.title: A Statistical Mechanics Framework for Emergent Capabilities in Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to develop a theoretical framework for emergent capabilities in foundation models by drawing an analogy from statistical physics, specifically the theory of phase transitions. The analogous problem is explaining how macroscopic properties (e.g., magnetization, fluidity) suddenly emerge in physical systems when microscopic parameters cross a critical point, solved using renormalization group (RG) theory. We adapt this solution by treating the training and scaling of foundation models as a process moving through a high-dimensional parameter space (model size, dataset size, compute). The goal is to create an RG-inspired theoretical framework that coarse-grains low-level model details, identifies fixed points and critical scaling laws in this parameter space, and provides a principled method to predict which capabilities will emerge at scale and at what thresholds, distinguishing between irrelevant details that are averaged out and relevant features that drive new emergent behaviors.

ideas.title: A Theory of Emergent Defects and Their Post-Hoc Correction
ideas.description: This research direction develops a unified theoretical framework to understand harmful emergent concepts—such as social biases, security vulnerabilities, or reasoning failures—as predictable consequences of scaling laws and training dynamics. The theory aims to map the 'latent defect manifolds' within a model's parameter space, explaining their origin and structure. This understanding then directly informs the development of targeted, post-training editing algorithms that can isolate and neutralize these emergent defects without catastrophic forgetting of other capabilities, enabling safer and more controllable scaling of foundation models.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sensor Reliability Modeling Using Biological Signal Processing Principles
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience and biological sensory processing. The analogous problem is how biological systems (e.g., vision, hearing) achieve robust perception despite inherently noisy and unreliable neural signals. The solution in nature involves population coding, redundancy, and Bayesian integration, where the brain weights signals from many neurons based on their estimated reliability and prior expectations. Adapted to the sensor modeling context, this leads to a research idea titled 'Adaptive Sensor Reliability Modeling Using Biological Signal Processing Principles.' The adapted idea proposes to model a sensor network not with static failure probabilities, but as a dynamic system. A Bayesian inference framework would continuously estimate each sensor's reliability based on its agreement with the population consensus and contextual priors (e.g., expected target behavior, environmental conditions). The system's fusion algorithm would then weight sensor inputs adaptively, dynamically de-emphasizing sensors behaving as outliers (potential failures or false alarms) and reinforcing those providing consistent, reliable data. This approach challenges the common assumption of fixed, known sensor error statistics and offers a more robust, self-correcting model for real-world applications where sensor reliability can change unpredictably.

ideas.title: Immunological Defenses for Foundation Models: Anomaly Detection and Response Inspired by the Human Immune System
ideas.description: This research investigates vulnerabilities like data poisoning in foundation model systems by drawing an analogy to immunological defenses in biology. The analogous solution from immunology involves a layered defense combining innate (general, fast) and adaptive (specific, learned) immunity, along with mechanisms like negative selection to distinguish self from non-self. The adapted idea proposes to design a security framework for foundation models that mimics this biological system. It will develop an 'innate immunity' component for broad, real-time anomaly detection in data streams, and an 'adaptive immunity' component that learns from encountered attacks to produce tailored defenses. A core mechanism, inspired by negative selection, will continuously train the system to better recognize legitimate data ('self'), thereby enhancing its sensitivity to malicious, out-of-distribution inputs ('non-self') used in poisoning and other integrity attacks, ultimately leading to more secure and private model training and deployment.

ideas.title: Robust Adaptation Under Sensor Uncertainty: A Benchmark for Evaluating Transfer Learning Alternatives
ideas.description: This research direction investigates whether standard fine-tuning from a pre-trained model remains the optimal adaptation strategy when deploying perception systems in environments with unreliable sensors prone to missed detections and false alarms. It posits that the knowledge embedded in models trained on clean, high-quality sensor data may be misaligned or even detrimental when directly transferred to a target domain defined by its specific sensor failure modes. The work will develop a benchmark where the domain shift is precisely characterized by sensor uncertainty parameters, enabling a systematic comparison of fine-tuning against alternative paradigms like robust retraining from scratch, uncertainty-aware architecture redesign, or hybrid model-based filtering. The goal is to establish principled guidelines for selecting adaptation strategies that prioritize robustness and safety over mere performance gains from potentially biased source knowledge.

ideas.title: Adversarial Sensor Placement via Game-Theoretic and Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of sensor placement under adversarial outbreaks by drawing an analogy from conservation biology, specifically the detection of strategically evasive invasive species. The analogous solution combines ecological niche modeling with game-theoretic optimization to place limited monitoring resources. Adapted to the context of adversarial contamination or information spread, the research proposes a Stackelberg game framework where a defender first places sensors on a network, and a rational adversary then initiates and guides an outbreak to maximize spread while minimizing detection, knowing the sensor layout. The solution involves developing algorithms to compute optimal or robust sensor placements by modeling the adversary's best-response using network propagation models and adversarial risk maps, effectively translating methods from ecological surveillance to cybersecurity and public health monitoring.

ideas.title: Targeted Neural Editing via Immunological Pruning
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from immunology to challenge the assumption that learned biases in AI models are irreversible without full retraining. In vaccinology, the immune system is selectively edited by exposing it to antigens, which triggers a targeted production of antibodies without erasing all prior immunity. The adapted idea proposes a method for 'Targeted Neural Editing via Immunological Pruning.' The core method involves designing and introducing synthetic 'antigen' data—counterexamples or adversarial prompts that specifically represent the harmful concept in a neutralizing context. A constrained, localized optimization process (akin to an immune response) would then be applied, using these antigens to selectively prune or dampen the synaptic weights and activations most responsible for the unwanted bias. The goal is to 'inoculate' the model, surgically removing or containing the harmful concept while causing minimal catastrophic interference to the rest of the model's capabilities, thereby editing defects post-training with high efficiency and specificity.

ideas.title: Noise-Baselined, Multi-Modal Detection of Directed Signal Propagation in Cellular Networks
ideas.description: This research develops a system to reliably detect micro-scale outbreak signals by first rigorously quantifying the inherent stochastic noise floor of the cellular network under study. It deploys paired physical and information-theoretic sensors—such as nanosensor arrays for metabolite flux and single-cell RNA sequencing for transcriptional waves—to capture potential propagation events. A causal inference engine then analyzes this multi-modal data to identify statistically significant, directed signal flows that are correlated with emergent phenotypic changes, effectively separating true network-level communication from background biological noise.

ideas.title: Inferring Outbreak Sources via Blind Deconvolution Methods
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a known prior distribution for outbreak sources by drawing an analogy from signal processing and seismology, specifically the problem of blind deconvolution. In that field, one must recover an original source signal and the system's impulse response using only the observed output, without prior knowledge of either. The analogous solution uses iterative statistical methods that impose general constraints (like sparsity) on both unknowns. Adapted to epidemiology, the research would develop methods to jointly infer the outbreak source distribution and key, uncertain transmission parameters (acting as the 'channel') from observed case data alone. This would replace reliance on a given source prior with a framework that learns both the sources and aspects of the transmission process simultaneously, constrained by general epidemiological structures such as source sparsity or spatial continuity, thereby making source inference more robust to incomplete data and uncertain prior knowledge.
