ideas.title: Decentralized Sensor Placement via Quorum Sensing and Morphogen Gradients
ideas.description: This research adapts self-organization principles from developmental biology—specifically quorum sensing and morphogen gradients—to the problem of decentralized sensor placement. In the proposed protocol, nodes propagate and measure simple, diffusing virtual signals that create gradients across the network, providing local positional context. Each node then uses a stochastic rule, based on the local concentration of this signal and the density of already-designated sensor neighbors (simulating a quorum), to self-select into the sensor role. The solution requires only local communication, avoids global coordination, and inherently adapts the sensor network's density and distribution to the network's scale and geometry, addressing the SCALING_DUAL dimension by balancing local decision-making with emergent global coverage.

ideas.title: Adversarially Robust, Adaptive Sensor Placement via Sequential Game-Theoretic Active Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a sequential decision-making framework for sensor placement where the placement agent (defender) and the contamination outbreak agent (adversary) are engaged in a repeated game. The defender employs active learning query strategies—such as those maximizing uncertainty or expected model change—to sequentially select the most informative sensor locations based on observed data. However, the adversary simultaneously adapts its outbreak strategy, using knowledge of the defender's past placements and learning algorithm to design evasive spread patterns. This creates a closed-loop, adaptive competition where the defender must not only learn the physical dynamics of spread but also infer and anticipate the adversary's evolving evasion tactics, leading to robust placement policies that are resilient to intelligent counter-detection efforts.

ideas.title: Behavioral Meta-Game Framework for Robust Adversarial Strategy Learning
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond classical equilibrium analysis by modeling the adversary as one of many possible behavioral types, each characterized by a reward function and a decision-making model (e.g., quantal response, learning algorithm). The system maintains a belief distribution over these types, which is updated via inverse reinforcement learning from sequential adversarial actions. The defender then optimizes a robust policy against this evolving belief distribution, ensuring adaptation to real-world adversaries who are not perfectly rational and whose goals may shift over time.

ideas.title: Governance-Aware Influence Minimization for AI Risk Detection
ideas.description: This research direction fuses influence minimization algorithms with interdisciplinary governance to create a dynamic system for the early detection and mitigation of societal risks from foundation models. It adapts network spread algorithms to minimize the time to detect the propagation of harmful outputs (e.g., misinformation, biased decisions), but crucially embeds this technical core within a collaborative governance structure. Legal scholars, ethicists, economists, and impacted communities continuously define the 'signals' to detect and shape the intervention strategies, ensuring they are legitimate and proportionate. This synergy yields a co-evolutionary tool where technical detection mechanisms are made ethically robust and socially informed, while governance processes gain precise, actionable early-warning capabilities.

ideas.title: Optimal Source Placement as a Quantum State Discrimination Problem
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from quantum information theory, specifically the problem of quantum state discrimination. In that field, given a fixed measurement apparatus, one seeks quantum states that are hardest to tell apart, maximizing the probability of measurement error. Adapted to the outbreak context, the sensor network placements are the fixed 'measurement.' The research problem becomes: find the optimal nodes to start an outbreak such that the resulting spatiotemporal infection patterns (analogous to quantum states) are minimally distinguishable by the sensor network, thereby maximizing the undetected time. This reframes the source-hiding inverse problem through the lens of maximizing the sensor network's discrimination error, potentially leveraging formalisms from quantum detection theory to identify source locations that exploit the fundamental limits of the sensor system's observational resolution.

ideas.title: Adaptive Seismic Array Design via Active Learning Query Strategies
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from geophysics, where optimal seismic array design sequentially places sensors to best resolve subsurface structures. The analogous solution uses model-based criteria to maximize information gain. This is adapted by directly employing active learning query strategies (like uncertainty sampling or expected model change) as the acquisition function for sequential sensor placement. Instead of selecting data points from a pool, the strategies now evaluate candidate physical locations, selecting the next sensor site that is predicted to be most informative for learning the target field or system model, thereby transferring the core AL methodology to a spatial experimental design problem.

ideas.title: Architectural Security for Foundation Models via Systemic Adversarial Simulation
ideas.description: This research moves beyond point defenses to develop a holistic architectural framework for securing foundation model systems. It creates a formal model of the end-to-end ML pipeline, specifying security contracts and trust boundaries between all stages from data ingestion to model serving. A core innovation is an automated adversarial simulation engine that continuously generates multi-stage, cross-component attack strategies to stress-test the entire architecture. The outcome is a principled methodology and toolset for designing inherently more robust systems, capable of resisting not just known attacks but also novel, systemic threats arising from component interactions.

ideas.title: Robust Optimization for Dynamic Network Epidemics (RODNE)
ideas.description: This research direction integrates robust optimization with dynamic network models to formulate optimal resource allocation (e.g., vaccines, lockdowns) for epidemic control. It treats both the disease transmission parameters and the potential evolution of the contact network (due to seasonal travel, policy shifts, or behavioral adaptation) as bounded uncertainties within a min-max regret framework. The novel synergy enables the derivation of policies that perform well across a family of plausible temporal network trajectories, rather than a single static topology or a simple parameter range, leading to more resilient public health planning.

ideas.title: Proactive Defect Tracing and Containment for Foundation Model Lineages
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for proactive defect containment in foundation model lineages. It creates a suite of diagnostic benchmarks that systematically trace specific failure modes—such as biases, vulnerabilities, or reasoning flaws—back to their root causes within a foundation model's architecture or training data. The core innovation is using these causal failure maps to generate targeted 'immunization' strategies, including specialized adapters, curated counterfactual training data, and architectural patches, which can be applied to downstream models during fine-tuning to block the inheritance of those specific defects. This moves beyond passive evaluation and generic mitigation, enabling precise, auditable interventions that increase the robustness and diversity of the entire model ecosystem derived from a common foundation.

ideas.title: Decentralized and Adaptive Sensor Role Management in Dynamic Networks
ideas.description: This research develops decentralized protocols for nodes to autonomously assume, maintain, and relinquish sensor roles based solely on local observations and neighbor communication. The system dynamically reallocates sensing responsibilities over time in response to node mobility, failures, changes in resource levels, or shifts in the threat model. This creates a network that self-organizes an optimal sensing posture, where the role of a 'sensor' is a temporary, transferable asset managed by a distributed consensus. The core challenge is designing lightweight algorithms that ensure stable, conflict-free role transitions and maintain coverage guarantees despite the lack of a global view.

ideas.title: Multi-Source Event Detection via Blind Source Separation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that surveillance systems must first disentangle or assume single-origin outbreaks for effective detection. Drawing an analogy from signal processing, where Blind Source Separation (BSS) isolates multiple independent audio sources from mixed recordings, we propose adapting these techniques to network epidemiology. The core research idea is to develop detection models that treat aggregated, network-wide anomaly signals as a mixture of several independent outbreak events originating at different nodes. By applying adapted BSS algorithms (e.g., variants of Independent Component Analysis tailored for spatio-temporal network data), the system would aim to separate the composite signal into its constituent outbreak 'sources,' estimating their origin locations, start times, and initial propagation patterns simultaneously. This enables detection and initial characterization of multiple concurrent outbreaks directly from the mixed surveillance stream, without requiring prior isolation of individual events.

ideas.title: Adversarially-Obfuscated Dynamic Sensor Placement with Guaranteed Coverage
ideas.description: This research develops algorithms for dynamic sensor redeployment that are explicitly robust to adversarial reconnaissance and exploitation. The core innovation is a game-theoretic framework where the placement optimizer directly competes against a simulated adversary model that learns from and predicts sensor movements. The system generates deployment strategies that are randomized and non-periodic, yet still provably satisfy a minimum coverage guarantee over time and space, preventing the creation of predictable blind spots. This results in a resilient adaptive system where dynamism enhances security rather than introducing a new attack surface.

ideas.title: Adaptive Network Epidemiology via Phase-Field Methods
ideas.description: This idea challenges the static network assumption in epidemic modeling by drawing an analogy from materials science, specifically phase-field modeling of microstructural evolution. In phase-field models, the internal topology of a material (like grain boundaries) changes dynamically based on energy landscapes, simulated via evolving order parameter fields on a fixed grid. The adapted solution applies this to epidemiology by representing network connectivity as a continuous field that co-evolves with compartmental disease states (S, I, R, etc.). The dynamics of the network field are driven by local 'energies' such as perceived risk, intervention policies, and behavioral adaptation, coupled to the infection prevalence. This results in a fully integrated model where the underlying contact network is not an input but an output, dynamically reshaping itself during the outbreak, offering a more realistic framework for modeling behavioral feedback and adaptive interventions.

ideas.title: Early Anomaly Detection in Cellular Networks via Seismic Aftershock Prediction Models
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodology from seismology's Epidemic-Type Aftershock Sequence (ETAS) models, used for early detection of propagating earthquake swarms, to the problem of micro-scale outbreak detection in biological networks. The analogous solution involves modeling stochastic, self-exciting spatio-temporal processes to distinguish signal propagation from background noise. The adapted idea applies this statistical framework to cellular or nanoscale networks, treating discrete anomalous biochemical signaling events (e.g., ion fluxes, protein modifications) as analogous to seismic events. By analyzing their rate, clustering, and propagation, the model aims to identify the nascent 'outbreak' of a pathological signaling cascade (like early metastasis or immune overreaction) at the microscale, long before it manifests as a detectable tissue-scale or organism-scale phenomenon, enabling ultra-early intervention.

ideas.title: Game-Theoretic Detection of Adversarial Pathogens in Cellular Communication Networks
ideas.description: This research fuses micro-scale signal detection in cellular networks with game-theoretic modeling to address the challenge of detecting intelligent, evasive pathogens or malfunctioning cellular processes. It models the interaction as a strategic game where a 'detector' deploys biosensors within a tissue or cellular network, while an 'evader' (e.g., a virus exploiting cell-to-cell signaling or a cancer cell modulating its microenvironment) dynamically alters its propagation path to avoid detection. The goal is to compute equilibrium strategies, revealing optimal, robust sensor placements and detection protocols that remain effective even against an adversary with knowledge of the network topology. This enables a proactive, strategic approach to early-warning systems in immunology, oncology, and synthetic biology that traditional passive detection methods cannot achieve.

ideas.title: Spatially-Grounded Bayesian Optimization for Sequential Sensor Placement
ideas.description: This approach frames optimal sensor placement as a Bayesian optimization problem over a physical domain, using a spatial probabilistic model (e.g., a Gaussian Process) to represent the underlying field of interest. Instead of directly transferring standard active learning heuristics, it employs acquisition functions derived from spatial statistics, such as entropy search or integrated variance reduction, to sequentially select locations that maximize the information gain about the entire spatial field. The system explicitly incorporates physical constraints and deployment costs into the acquisition function, ensuring recommendations are practical. This results in a robust, principled strategy for placing sensors that directly minimizes global prediction uncertainty in spatially correlated phenomena.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sensor Array Optimization via Real-Time Data Assimilation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core solution from Numerical Weather Prediction—real-time data assimilation—to the problem of combining optimal sensor placement with online learning. In meteorology, models continuously ingest heterogeneous sensor data (like satellite radiances) to correct forecasts and guide future observation targeting. Similarly, for a sensor network deployed for detection (e.g., of contaminants, threats, or faults), the pre-computed optimal placement provides the initial 'forecast' of the detection field. An ensemble-based data assimilation algorithm would then integrate incoming, possibly noisy, real-time sensor readings to update a probabilistic detection state. Crucially, the analysis step computes the reduction in uncertainty, which feeds back to recommend adaptive sensor management—such as adjusting sampling rates, toggling sensors on/off, or suggesting minor mechanical re-aiming—to optimally prepare for the next assimilation cycle, thereby closing the loop between placement, real-time data, and adaptive resource allocation.

ideas.title: Adapting Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) for AI Model Robustness Benchmarks
ideas.description: This research adapts the systematic risk assessment methodology of Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) from Systems Engineering to the evaluation of AI model robustness. The analogous solution involves proactively cataloging potential failures, their causes, and effects to prioritize mitigation. Adapted to AI, the idea is to create a formalized benchmark framework that first establishes a comprehensive taxonomy of AI failure modes (categorizing vulnerabilities like spurious correlations, out-of-distribution performance drops, and security exploits). For each failure mode, the framework would define metrics for severity (impact of the failure) and likelihood (probability under specified conditions), allowing for the calculation of a risk priority score. The core output would be a suite of evaluation benchmarks and a standardized method to apply them, enabling systematic, comparative assessment of where and how models fail, moving beyond average performance to a rigorous analysis of failure boundaries and critical weaknesses.

ideas.title: Context-Adaptive, Feedback-Driven Sensor Placement for Evolving Multi-Objective Detection
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic sensor placement and management system that overcomes the static nature of traditional multicriteria optimization. The system continuously monitors detection performance against a set of core objectives and ingests streams of contextual data, including new threat alerts and mission changes, to dynamically reweight objective priorities. Using this adaptive utility model, it employs a mixture of physical actuator control (for movable sensors) and algorithmic resource allocation (e.g., dwell time, mode switching) to virtually reconfigure the sensor network, optimizing for the current operational context. This results in a resilient surveillance infrastructure that maintains Pareto-optimal performance even as objectives conflict and evolve over time.

ideas.title: Compressive Sensing for Time-Guaranteed Sparse Event Detection
ideas.description: This research fuses compressive sensing theory with temporal performance guarantees to design sparse sensor networks for event detection. The core idea is to model the appearance and spread of an event (like a leak or intrusion) over a spatial domain as a time-varying sparse signal. By strategically placing a minimal set of sensors whose measurements form a suitable sensing matrix, the system can reconstruct the event's location from few samples. The novel synergy lies in rigorously deriving the conditions on sensor placement and sampling rate required by compressive sensing theory to ensure the event is always reconstructed—and thus detected—within a pre-specified, strict time bound, which neither pure optimization nor pure sensing approaches alone can provide.

ideas.title: Runtime Immune System for Emergent Defects in Foundation Model Derivatives
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to detect and correct latent, emergent defects in models derived from foundation models after deployment. Instead of relying solely on pre-training audits, it creates a distributed monitoring network that uses consensus mechanisms and anomaly detection to identify when downstream models begin exhibiting homogenized faulty behaviors. Upon detection, the system triggers localized model patching, resource isolation, or advisory ensemble voting to contain the defect, creating a dynamic defense against vulnerabilities that only become apparent in production.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sensor Repositioning via Optimal Foraging Theory
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from Optimal Foraging Theory in Behavioral Ecology to the problem of dynamic sensor placement. In ecology, animals efficiently forage in dynamic environments using strategies like the Marginal Value Theorem and adaptive movement patterns (e.g., Lévy flights) to balance exploration and exploitation. The adapted idea models the sensor network as a foraging agent, where 'resources' are information gains. An algorithm will be developed for sensors to autonomously decide when to reposition by comparing their local marginal information yield to the network's global average, while using strategically randomized, biologically-inspired movement patterns to optimally cover evolving threat landscapes and network structures.

ideas.title: Dynamic Sensor Orchestration for Resilient Time-Bounded Detection
ideas.description: This research develops a system that maintains a strict detection time bound in dynamic, uncertain environments by continuously adapting the sensor network. Instead of finding a single minimal set, it uses a resilient pool of heterogeneous sensors and actively monitors their performance, environmental conditions, and potential adversary behaviors. A runtime assurance layer dynamically reallocates sensing resources and tasks redundant assets to compensate for failures or degraded performance. The outcome is a robust, self-correcting surveillance system that provides a statistically guaranteed detection time despite sensor faults, environmental changes, or adaptive threats.

ideas.title: Adversarial Simulation and Stress-Testing for Actionable AI Governance Prototypes
ideas.description: This research program structures interdisciplinary collaboration to produce and validate specific, implementable governance tools for foundation models. Teams of ethicists, legal scholars, economists, and engineers are tasked with co-developing concrete governance prototypes, such as real-time compliance monitors or novel liability contracts. These prototypes are then subjected to rigorous, evidence-based stress-testing in simulated adversarial scenarios designed by opposing teams, which must attempt to break or game the proposed mechanisms. The process forces convergence on robust, actionable solutions backed by documented performance under pressure, moving beyond theoretical discussion to generate compelling evidence for policymakers and industry adopters.

ideas.title: Apply Radio Interferometry Image Reconstruction for Sparse Event Detection
ideas.description: This idea frames detection as reconstructing a sparse spatial signal (events) from few sensor measurements, directly analogous to reconstructing an image of sparse celestial sources from the incomplete Fourier data captured by a radio interferometer. The analogous solution from radio astronomy involves specialized algorithms like CLEAN or compressed sensing variants that solve this ill-posed inverse problem by exploiting sparsity. Adapted to monitoring, the sensor network is conceptualized as a sparse interferometric array. The measurements are processed not just for individual sensor alerts but are combined to solve for a full spatial 'map' of activity, enabling precise localization and separation of multiple sparse events—such as security breaches or equipment failures—far beyond the resolution limit of the physical sensor density.

ideas.title: Adapting Predator-Prey Coevolutionary Dynamics for Adversarial Detector Placement
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to modeling predator-prey coevolutionary arms races from evolutionary ecology to the problem of adversarial detector placement. In ecology, the Red Queen hypothesis and evolutionary game theory are used in simulations where predator and prey populations evolve counter-strategies over generations, revealing evolutionary stable strategies (ESS) and cyclic dynamics. By modeling the detector as the 'predator' and the evader as the 'prey', we can implement a coevolutionary algorithm where populations of placement strategies and evasion strategies compete in tournaments. Their fitness determines their reproduction into the next generation. Running this simulation allows us to analyze the emergent equilibrium strategies, identify robust detector placements that perform well against a wide range of evolved evasions, and understand the dynamic nature of the security arms race, leading to more resilient system designs.

ideas.title: Game-Theoretic Governance for Adversarial AI Societal Impact
ideas.description: This research integrates game theory and interdisciplinary governance studies to model the societal impact of foundation models as a dynamic, multi-player game involving developers, regulators, users, and adversarial evaders. It moves beyond static ethical assessments by using equilibrium analysis to predict how governance rules (e.g., disclosure laws, safety standards) will influence the strategic behavior of all parties in adversarial scenarios like disinformation or automated fraud. The outcome is a computational toolkit for policymakers to simulate and compare proposed governance regimes, identifying robust rules that remain effective even under strategic evasion, thereby closing the gap between high-level ethical principles and enforceable, strategically sound regulation.

ideas.title: A Mechanistic and Predictive Scaling Theory for Emergent Capabilities via Controlled Experimentation
ideas.description: This research program develops falsifiable theoretical frameworks for emergence by tightly coupling hypothesis generation with controlled, multi-factor scaling experiments. Instead of analyzing existing large models post-hoc, it designs a series of coordinated model families trained across a structured grid of scaling variables (compute, data, architecture, parameter count). The core theoretical output is a set of mechanistic explanations that must predict specific, measurable capability transitions in held-out regions of the scaling manifold, validated through targeted model interventions and ablation studies. This approach moves beyond description to establish causal, predictive theories of emergence.

ideas.title: Cost-Decoupled Decentralized Sensor Placement with Sybil Resistance
ideas.description: This protocol enables nodes to self-select as sensors using local information while being robust to Sybil attacks. Each node must compute a verifiable, resource-intensive proof-of-work puzzle whose solution serves as a ticket for election, with the puzzle difficulty auto-calibrating to local node density. The election mechanism uses these tickets in a local consensus round to ensure a spatially distributed and non-colluding set of sensors is selected. This creates a stable, attack-resistant sensor network where influence is proportional to real, non-forgeable resource expenditure.

ideas.title: Apply Robust Portfolio Optimization from Finance to Epidemiological Spread Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts robust portfolio optimization from financial portfolio management to handle uncertainty in epidemiological spread models. In finance, min-max regret robust optimization is used to construct investment portfolios that perform acceptably well across a wide range of uncertain future market conditions, rather than optimizing for a single forecast. The analogous solution is adapted by conceptualizing public health interventions (vaccines, therapeutics, NPIs) as a portfolio of 'investments' against an epidemic. The core uncertainty in the spread parameters (e.g., R0, generation interval, hospitalization rate) is captured by a defined uncertainty set. The optimization problem then seeks the allocation of limited resources (doses, funds, personnel) that minimizes the maximum possible regret—the difference in outcomes between the chosen allocation and the optimal allocation one would have chosen if the true parameters were known in advance—across all parameter scenarios within the set. This yields a resource allocation strategy robust to the inherent uncertainty in modeling disease spread.

ideas.title: Pareto-Optimal Sensor Placement via Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithms
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to multi-criteria optimization from evolutionary engineering design to the problem of sensor placement. The analogous field is Evolutionary Computation for Engineering Design, where Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithms (MOEAs) like NSGA-II are used to find Pareto-optimal solutions when objectives like weight, strength, and cost conflict. The adapted idea applies NSGA-II to sensor placement, encoding potential sensor layouts and defining multiple, potentially conflicting detection objectives (e.g., coverage, cost, resilience) as separate fitness functions. The algorithm will output a set of Pareto-optimal placement strategies, providing a clear trade-off frontier from which a system designer can select the most suitable configuration based on overarching mission requirements, thereby extending sensor placement methodology to handle multiple objectives simultaneously without collapsing them into a single weighted sum.

ideas.title: Game-Theoretic Security for Foundation Models: Proactive Defense Against Strategic Adversaries
ideas.description: This research synthesizes game theory and security/privacy analysis to develop a dynamic, strategic defense framework for foundation model systems. It models the ongoing interaction between a system defender and strategic adversaries (e.g., conducting data poisoning, extraction, or evasion attacks) as a multi-stage game with imperfect information, where actions in training affect deployment vulnerabilities. The goal is to compute or approximate equilibrium strategies that make the foundation model system robust by design, forcing adversaries into less harmful actions or making attacks economically non-viable. This leads to systems that can anticipate and adapt to evolving threats, providing a principled methodology for allocating security resources and hardening models against the most probable strategic attacks.

ideas.title: A Precautionary, Adaptive Governance Framework for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research seed adapts the governance solution pioneered in synthetic biology to the context of foundation models. The analogous problem was managing the profound societal and ethical risks of a powerful, dual-use technology (genetic engineering) before its full implications were known, solved through a precautionary, collaborative, and adaptive governance process. The adapted idea proposes to foster deep collaboration across fields by instituting a structured, interdisciplinary mechanism mirroring the Asilomar process. This would involve convening task forces from AI, law, ethics, economics, and social sciences during a deliberate planning phase for frontier models. Their goal would be to co-create a dynamic governance framework featuring standardized risk assessments, verifiable safety protocols, and tiered guidelines that proactively address ethical, legal, and economic impacts, thereby translating interdisciplinary insight into actionable governance structures.
